confirmed.title: Backend Docker build is broken: it copies only the root pointer requirements.txt whose `-r backend/requirements.txt` target is not yet in the image, and pins python:3.11 vs the 3.14.4 runtime
confirmed.claim: docker-compose builds the backend with context `.` and dockerfile backend/Dockerfile. That Dockerfile does `COPY requirements.txt .` then `pip install -r requirements.txt` BEFORE `COPY . .` — but the root requirements.txt is now only a pointer containing `-r backend/requirements.txt` (pip resolves nested -r relative to the referencing file), so /app/backend/requirements.txt does not exist at that layer and the pip layer fails: `docker compose build backend` cannot succeed. Independently, the image pins python:3.11-slim while the system runs Python 3.14.4 — even if fixed, the container resolves and runs a different interpreter/resolution than the audited environment (slack_bot/Dockerfile shares the 3.11 pin but copies the correct file).
confirmed.evidence:
backend/Dockerfile:1-9 'FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app

COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --upgrade pip && \
    pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

COPY . .'. requirements.txt:6 '-r backend/requirements.txt'. docker-compose.yml backend: 'build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: backend/Dockerfile'. Contrast backend/slack_bot/Dockerfile: 'COPY backend/requirements.txt ./backend/requirements.txt'.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T9 reproducibility rules + T2 ('Repo runs Python 3.14.4'); pip requirements-file semantics (nested -r resolved relative to the containing file, pip.pypa.io/en/stable/reference/requirements-file-format/).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Either fix the Dockerfile (`COPY backend/requirements.txt ./backend/requirements.txt` + install from it, and bump base image to python:3.14-slim to match the audited runtime) or, if the Docker path is confirmed dormant, delete/quarantine docker-compose.yml + backend/Dockerfile so a broken deployment artifact isn't carried as if it worked.
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified: backend/Dockerfile:5 copies root requirements.txt (now only a pointer containing '-r backend/requirements.txt') and runs pip install at lines 6-7 BEFORE 'COPY . .' at line 9. Pip resolves nested -r relative to the containing file, so it looks for /app/backend/requirements.txt, which does not exist at that layer — the pip layer fails and 'docker compose build backend' cannot succeed. docker-compose.yml confirms context '.' + dockerfile backend/Dockerfile; backend/slack_bot/Dockerfile shows the correct COPY pattern, confirming the backend Dockerfile is the outlier. The python:3.11-slim pin vs the audited 3.14.4 runtime (research_brief_75.0.md T2) is also real. No guard/fallback neutralizes it; not in phase-69/70/71/72/73 registers (docker grep across audit_phase69, confirmed_findings.json, design docs returned nothing). Docker path appears dormant (system runs from local .venv), so P2 debt is the honest severity.

confirmed.title: Frontend Dockerfile ignores the committed lockfile: copies only package.json and runs `npm install`, so every image build does a fresh unpinned resolution
confirmed.claim: The deps stage copies package.json WITHOUT package-lock.json and runs `npm install`, discarding the 507KB committed lockfile's guarantees: every image build re-resolves all 24 caret-ranged prod deps to latest-in-range at build time. This is exactly the class the npm-lock docs and the 2025-26 npm compromise wave (chalk/debug/axios, Shai-Hulud) warn about, and during a window like Dec 2025 a build from `next ^15.0.0` would have taken whatever 15.x npm served rather than the verified-patched 15.5.12 in the lockfile. CI already does this correctly (e2e-smoke.yml:84 and visual-regression.yml:55 run `npm ci`), so the Docker path is the odd one out.
confirmed.evidence:
frontend/Dockerfile:1-4 'FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json ./
RUN npm install'. frontend/package.json:35-36 '"next": "^15.0.0",
    "next-auth": "^5.0.0-beta.30"'. Lockfile present: frontend/package-lock.json (507165 bytes, lockfileVersion 3, root entry matches package.json exactly — verified no drift). grep 'npm ci' in repo: only .github/workflows/e2e-smoke.yml:84 and visual-regression.yml:55.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T9 ('npm ci (not npm install) for reproduction'; 'teammates, deployments, and continuous integration are guaranteed to install exactly the same dependencies') and recency #9 npm supply-chain escalation; https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/commands/npm-ci.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Change the deps stage to `COPY package.json package-lock.json ./` + `RUN npm ci` (and consider `--omit=dev` in a separate prod-deps stage). If the Docker frontend path is dormant, retire it together with deps-03.
confirmed.verify_reason: Evidence verified exactly: frontend/Dockerfile:3-4 copies only package.json and runs npm install despite a committed 507KB lockfileVersion-3 package-lock.json; no .dockerignore or version pinning neutralizes it, and docker-compose.yml:21-24 actively builds this Dockerfile as the frontend service, so every image build re-resolves caret ranges (next ^15.0.0 etc.) unpinned. CI correctly uses npm ci (e2e-smoke.yml:84, visual-regression.yml:55), making the Docker path the inconsistent one. Not fixed (Dockerfile untouched since 2026-03-08), not sanctioned by .claude/rules/frontend.md, and no phase-69/70/71/72/73 register item covers it. P2 stands: the operator runs the frontend via next dev so the Docker path is dormant day-to-day (not P0/P1), but it is a wired deployment path one command from live with genuine supply-chain/reproducibility exposure and a one-line fix.

confirmed.title: Auth-critical next-auth is pinned to a beta (5.0.0-beta.30) of a library now in maintenance mode under new maintainership
confirmed.claim: The production auth path (Google SSO + WebAuthn + JWE session tokens that the FastAPI backend decrypts manually via HKDF) runs on `next-auth ^5.0.0-beta.30`, resolved to exactly 5.0.0-beta.30 in the lockfile. Per the research gate, Auth.js v5 has a stable line and the project 'is now part of Better Auth' in security/maintenance mode with new projects directed elsewhere — so the repo sits on a pre-stable beta of a sunsetting library at its single most security-sensitive seam. Because the backend re-implements the JWE decrypt contract (HKDF info=b"Auth.js Generated Encryption Key"), any forced future bump (e.g., a security release) risks a silent token-format/claims change that must be re-verified against backend/api/auth.py — this is planned-migration debt, not a mechanical bump.
confirmed.evidence: frontend/package.json:36 '"next-auth": "^5.0.0-beta.30",'. frontend/package-lock.json:10405-10407 '"node_modules/next-auth": {' ... '"resolved": "https://registry.npmjs.org/next-auth/-/next-auth-5.0.0-beta.30.tgz"'. .claude/rules/security.md: 'NextAuth.js v5 JWE tokens decrypted via HKDF(AUTH_SECRET, info=b"Auth.js Generated Encryption Key", ...)'.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md recency #10 ('v5 stable ... maintained by the Better Auth team (2026) in security/maintenance mode; new projects officially directed to Better Auth — repo's next-auth ^5.0.0-beta.30 pin is BEHIND the stable line AND on a sunsetting library') + snippet rows S11/S12 (authjs.dev/getting-started/migrating-to-v5; github.com/nextauthjs/next-auth/discussions/13252).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Plan a two-step: (1) bump beta.30 -> latest v5 stable with an explicit regression test of the backend JWE decrypt path (login roundtrip + backend get_current_user against a fresh token); (2) open a tracked decision on Better Auth migration timing. Do not leave the auth stack on an unmaintained beta indefinitely.
confirmed.verify_reason: Evidence verified exactly: frontend/package.json:36 pins next-auth ^5.0.0-beta.30, lockfile resolves 5.0.0-beta.30 (package-lock.json:10406). The backend-coupling claim is real: backend/api/auth.py hand-rolls the Auth.js JWE key derivation (HKDF with info "Auth.js Generated Encryption Key ({salt})" at line 56) and already carries a _hkdf_derive_key_v4 legacy path (line 60) proving the token contract has drifted across versions before — so a forced security bump is not mechanical. External status (v5 stable line; Auth.js in maintenance mode under Better Auth) is grounded in the phase research gate (research_brief_75.0.md recency #10, snippet S12). No intentional-pin documentation in CLAUDE.md/.claude/rules; no duplicate in phase 69-73 registers (audit_phase69 register/confirmed.json and current handoff files have zero next-auth items). P2 is the honest severity: planned-migration debt at the auth seam, not a live defect (lockfile keeps it reproducible; the brief itself calls it a maintenance-risk datapoint, not an emergency).

confirmed.title: Advisory scanning is backend-only: no npm audit workflow and no Dependabot config for the frontend tree
confirmed.claim: The only dependency-vulnerability wiring in the repo is pip-audit.yml, scoped to backend/requirements.txt (push/PR/weekly). There is zero npm-side equivalent: no workflow runs `npm audit`, and .github/ has no dependabot.yml — so advisories against the resolved frontend tree (next 15.5.12, next-auth beta, react 19.2.4, prisma 6.19.2, 500KB lock of transitives) are caught only if someone happens to look. The CVE-2025-66478 episode (patch state had to be manually verified during research) shows the discovery cost; per the brief the December exposure-window question is still open for the security auditor.
confirmed.evidence: .github/workflows/pip-audit.yml:45-50 'Run pip-audit (strict)\n        run: |\n          pip-audit \\\n            --requirement backend/requirements.txt \\\n            --strict'. grep -rn 'npm audit' across .github/workflows/: zero hits (only 'npm ci' in e2e-smoke.yml:84, visual-regression.yml:55). ls -a .github/: 'agents CODEOWNERS copilot-instructions.md instructions prompts workflows' — no dependabot.yml.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T9 rule 3 ('pip-audit / npm audit runs ... a scheduled advisory scan is a cheap step') and recency #9 (Sonatype 2026 +75% YoY malicious packages; 7-day cooldown/npm ci best practices); npm-audit docs https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/commands/npm-audit. Masterplan 4.8.8 (pip-audit, DONE) is not re-litigated — this is the uncovered npm half.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add a sibling npm-audit.yml (weekly + on package-lock.json change): `npm ci && npm audit --audit-level=high` in frontend/, artifact on failure — mirroring pip-audit.yml's shape. Optionally add dependabot.yml (npm + pip + github-actions ecosystems, weekly, with a cooldown window per the brief's 7-day guidance).
confirmed.verify_reason: All evidence verified: pip-audit.yml:45-50 matches the quote and is scoped to backend/requirements.txt + root requirements.txt only; grep across .github/workflows/ finds zero `npm audit` usage (only `npm ci` in e2e-smoke and visual-regression); .github/ has no dependabot.yml. Frontend tree exists as claimed (next ^15, next-auth ^5.0.0-beta.30, react ^19, prisma ^6.19.2, 496K package-lock.json). Not already fixed and not queued: harness_log.md:10724/10728 records a prior QA verdict that npm audit found 1 critical/6 high pre-existing vulns 'flagged for separate hygiene cycle', but that is a one-time `npm audit fix` note with no register id or masterplan step — no phase-69/70/71/72/73 register item or masterplan entry covers CI-wired npm advisory scanning or Dependabot. That episode (vulns only discovered incidentally during a Next.js bump QA) actually corroborates the discovery-cost claim. P2 is the honest severity: process/tooling debt, not a live defect.

confirmed.title: Cloud Functions requirements are fully unpinned and conflict with backend floors, on a committed python311 deploy path outside all audit coverage
confirmed.claim: functions/{ingestion,quant}/requirements.txt declare completely unversioned deps (google-cloud-bigquery, pandas, numpy, pyarrow, pytz, yfinance, functions-framework, google-cloud-storage) and functions/earnings uses weaker floors than backend (requests>=2.25.0 vs backend requests>=2.31.0) — direct violation of the repo's own phase-3.7.6 pin-hardening policy and a cross-file constraint conflict. scripts/deploy/deploy_agents.sh still deploys all four functions with --runtime=python311; any re-deploy resolves latest-of-everything (pandas 3.x/numpy 2.x/yfinance 1.x breaking-change majors) at deploy time, and pip-audit.yml's paths filter never scans these files. Last git touch is the 2026-04-13 restructure, so the surface is plausibly dormant — which argues for retirement rather than silent carriage.
confirmed.evidence:
functions/ingestion/requirements.txt: 'google-cloud-bigquery
functions-framework ...
pandas
numpy
pyarrow ...
pytz ...
yfinance'. functions/earnings/requirements.txt: 'requests>=2.25.0

Major project restructure'.

confirmed.verify_reason: All quoted evidence verified byte-for-byte: functions/ingestion and functions/quant requirements are fully unversioned; functions/earnings requests>=2.25.0 conflicts with backend requests>=2.31.0 (backend/requirements.txt:21); deploy_agents.sh deploys all four functions with --runtime=python311 (lines 147-150, 171-174, 196-199, 221-225); pip-audit.yml paths/--requirement cover only backend/requirements.txt and root requirements.txt, never functions/*; last functions/ touch is the 2026-04-13 restructure. No guard or wrapper neutralizes the unpinned resolve-at-deploy path, and no phase-69/70/71/72/73 register item (confirmed/contested/refuted/design_pack_73) covers this surface. Violation of the repo's own phase-3.7.6 pin-hardening policy documented in backend/requirements.txt. confirmed.title: fpdf2 is declared but imported nowhere in backend or scripts
confirmed.claim: `fpdf2>=2.7.0` (comment: '# PDF generation') has zero import sites: grep for `import fpdf`/`from fpdf`/`FPDF(` across backend/ and scripts/ returns nothing, so it is dead declared surface that still gets installed, resolved and (weekly) pip-audited for no benefit — pure supply-chain attack/maintenance surface. Contrast xlrd, which also has zero direct imports but IS legitimately required as pandas' .xls engine for macro_regime.py:154 reading data_gpr_export.xls (keep xlrd; drop or justify fpdf2).
confirmed.evidence: backend/requirements.txt:25-26 '# PDF generation\nfpdf2>=2.7.0'. grep -rE 'import fpdf|from fpdf|FPDF\(' backend scripts --include='*.py': zero hits; the only PDF/excel-adjacent hit is backend/services/macro_regime.py:154 'df = pd.read_excel(_GPR_CACHE_PATH)' with macro_regime.py:59 '_GPR_CACHE_PATH = _GPR_CACHE_DIR / "data_gpr_export.xls"' (that justifies xlrd, not fpdf2). pip list: fpdf2 2.8.7 installed.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T9 (every declared dep is supply-chain surface; 'floors+lockfile rather than naked floors') + role charter 'declared but unused'.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Remove `fpdf2>=2.7.0` from backend/requirements.txt (git-archaeology first: if a planned PDF feature owns it, move it to that feature's step; otherwise delete). Add an inline comment on xlrd noting it is a pandas read_excel engine dep so the next unused-dep sweep doesn't remove it.
confirmed.verify_reason: Confirmed. backend/requirements.txt:26 declares fpdf2>=2.7.0 under '# PDF generation'; repo-wide grep for 'import fpdf|from fpdf|FPDF(' across backend/ and scripts/ returns zero hits. Only usage is docs/archive/pyfinagent-app/components/sidebar.py:3 (archived legacy Streamlit app's PDF export), and git -S shows fpdf2 entered via commit 26af4ff7 'new frontend' — a carryover from that dead app, never used by the live backend. Nothing neutralizes it (no dynamic import, no plugin loading). xlrd contrast is accurate and already has an inline comment (line 20) justifying it as the GPR .xls engine, so only the fpdf2 removal remains. No duplicate in phase-69/70/71/72/73 registers (no fpdf mention in handoff/current). P3 severity honest: dead declared supply-chain surface, no live correctness impact.

confirmed.title: Directly-imported packages (numpy, PyYAML, pytest, google-cloud-storage, dateutil) are undeclared, riding on other packages' transitive closures
confirmed.claim: Backend code imports numpy (metrics/sortino.py, tools/monte_carlo.py, tools/anomaly_detector.py, backtest modules), yaml (5+ modules incl. governance/limits_schema.py, intel/source_registry.py, meta_evolution/*), pytest (127 test files; only pytest-cov is pinned), python-dateutil, and google-cloud-storage (compliance_logger WORM writer + earnings_tone) — none declared in backend/requirements.txt. All currently arrive transitively (pandas→numpy, uvicorn[standard]→PyYAML, pytest-cov→pytest, google-cloud-aiplatform→google-cloud-storage), so an upstream extras/deps change (e.g., uvicorn dropping PyYAML from `standard`, or the pinned aiplatform being removed) breaks these modules — notably the compliance WORM-audit path, whose `from google.cloud import storage` at _write_gcs has no ImportError guard.
confirmed.evidence:
backend/governance/limits_schema.py:22 'import yaml'. backend/metrics/sortino.py:29 'import numpy as np'. backend/services/compliance_logger.py:120-123 'def _write_gcs(record: RationaleRecord) -> str:
    bucket_name = os.environ["COMPLIANCE_WORM_BUCKET"]
    from google.cloud import storage    # type: ignore
    client = storage.Client()' (same at :141). grep of backend/requirements.txt for numpy|yaml|^pytest|storage|dateutil matches only 'google-cloud-bigquery-storage>=2.24.0' and 'pytest-cov==7.1.0' — none of the five declared.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T9 (reproducibility discipline) + role charter 'deps imported but not declared'; standard packaging best practice: declare every direct import, never rely on transitive availability (implicit in the brief's floors+lockfile rule and the uv compile workflow #11).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add explicit entries: numpy>=<tested>, PyYAML>=6.0, pytest (pinned major, beside pytest-cov), python-dateutil, google-cloud-storage>=<tested> to backend/requirements.txt — one-line each, resolution unchanged since all are already installed; the lockfile from deps-01 then pins them.
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified: backend/requirements.txt (and the root pointer file; no pyproject/setup.py/dev-requirements exist) declares none of numpy, PyYAML, pytest, python-dateutil, google-cloud-storage, yet all five are directly imported in production code — numpy in 10+ modules, yaml in 5, dateutil in backtest/walk_forward.py:8, and an unguarded 'from google.cloud import storage' at compliance_logger.py:122/141 (WORM audit path) and earnings_tone.py:38/56. All arrive only via transitive closures (pandas→numpy, uvicorn[standard]→PyYAML, pytest-cov→pytest, aiplatform→storage), so an upstream extras/deps change breaks them at import/call time. Not in the phase-69 register; distinct from the sibling deps-01 lockfile finding (declaring direct deps vs pinning resolution are complementary). P3 is honest: no live breakage, purely latent packaging hygiene.

confirmed.title: GET /api/mas/dashboard runs sync subprocess + sync httpx inline in async def (up to ~35s event-loop stall)
confirmed.claim: The async def get_dashboard route executes two blocking subprocess.run calls (timeout=10 and timeout=15) and a synchronous httpx.Client call (list_openclaw_sessions, timeout=10.0) directly on the event loop; a slow/hung openclaw CLI or gateway stalls the ENTIRE single-process trading backend (all HTTP requests + the in-process APScheduler-driven paper-trading loop share this loop) for up to ~35s per dashboard load.
confirmed.evidence:
mas_events.py:88-89 `@router.get("/dashboard")` / `async def get_dashboard():`; :143 `import subprocess, json as _json`; :146-149 `gw = subprocess.run(
 ["openclaw", "gateway", "status"],
 capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=10,
 )`; :153-156 `cron_out = subprocess.run(
 ["openclaw", "cron", "list", "--json"],
 capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=15,
 )`; :174 `oc_sessions = list_openclaw_sessions()` — which is sync `with httpx.Client(timeout=10.0) as client:` (backend/agents/openclaw_client.py:221-224). No to_thread anywhere in the route.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T1 rule 1 (async def bodies calling sync SDKs = 'the No.1 FastAPI production defect class'); https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/async/ ('blocking I/O... would block the server'); project rule .claude/rules/backend-api.md §Async safety ('Never call sync I/O... directly inside async def endpoints — use await asyncio.to_thread'; 'Blocking the event loop causes all concurrent HTTP requests to hang').
confirmed.suggested_fix: Wrap the two subprocess.run calls and list_openclaw_sessions() in await asyncio.to_thread(...) (or gather them concurrently); alternatively make the OpenClaw block a cached background probe like cron_dashboard's _launchctl_state TTL cache.
confirmed.verify_reason: All quoted evidence verified at the stated lines: async def get_dashboard (mas_events.py:88-89) runs subprocess.run(["openclaw","gateway","status"], timeout=10) at 146-149, subprocess.run(["openclaw","cron","list","--json"], timeout=15) at 153-156, and sync list_openclaw_sessions() at 174 which uses sync httpx.Client(timeout=10.0) (openclaw_client.py:224) — all directly on the event loop with no to_thread/executor/cache. In context the impact claim holds: the router is mounted (main.py:500) and the backend runs AsyncIOScheduler on the same loop (main.py:266-267, 310-315; slack_bot/scheduler.py:224), so a hung openclaw CLI/gateway stalls all HTTP traffic and the in-process paper-trading scheduler for up to ~35s per dashboard load; even healthy loads block for CLI execution time. The same route correctly uses httpx.AsyncClient for its earlier health checks (116-126), confirming the sync tail is an oversight, and it directly violates .claude/rules/backend-api.md §Async safety. Not fixed, not intentional, and not a duplicate — phase-69 register.md line 58 flags the same defect class but in backend/agents/orchestrator.py, a different route; no register item covers mas_events/get_dashboard. P1 is honest: not P0 (requires a slow/hung external CLI plus a dashboard load; stalls rather than corrupts state) but latent-serious given the route is hit by Slack App Home and the frontend /agents page and freezes the trading loop.

confirmed.title: GET /api/perf/llm/p95 executes a raw sync BigQuery query with no timeout inside async def — unbounded event-loop hang
confirmed.claim: async def get_llm_p95_latency calls bq.client.query(sql).result() synchronously on the event loop with NO timeout and no to_thread; the harness tab polls this endpoint, and a BQ network drop (the documented python-bigquery hang class) freezes every request and the in-process trading scheduler indefinitely — the surrounding except only catches raised exceptions, not a hang.
confirmed.evidence: performance_api.py:49-50 `@router.get("/llm/p95")` / `async def get_llm_p95_latency(`; :81-82 `# BigQueryClient exposes .client for raw queries.\n        rows = list(bq.client.query(sql).result())` — contrast the same file's compliant sibling cost_budget_api.py:88 `rows = list(client.query(sql, timeout=30).result())` called via `await asyncio.to_thread(...)` (cost_budget_api.py:126,141).
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T1 rule 1 + T4 rule 3 ('Every client.query(...) and .result(...) carries a timeout (repo convention 30s)') + snippet S9 googleapis/python-bigquery#2310 hang-on-network-drop; https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/async/; .claude/rules/backend-api.md §Async safety explicitly names BigQuery.
confirmed.suggested_fix: rows = await asyncio.to_thread(lambda: list(bq.client.query(sql, timeout=30).result(timeout=30))) — matching the repo's 30s convention and the cost_budget_api pattern.
confirmed.verify_reason: Defect is real: performance_api.py:82 runs bq.client.query(sql).result() with no timeout and no to_thread inside async def get_llm_p95_latency, violating .claude/rules/backend-api.md async-safety (which names BigQuery) and diverging from cost_budget_api.py's timeout=30/to_thread pattern; except only catches raised errors, not a hang. Not a duplicate (phase-69 event-loop item is orchestrator.py:1810, different site). However the P1 aggravator is false: no frontend or backend code calls /api/perf/llm/p95 (grep of frontend/src finds only summary/cache/optimize consumers; the 'harness tab consumes this' comment is stale), and the endpoint never passes its built query params to client.query(), so real calls error out at BQ and return unavailable — the route is effectively unwired. Blocking hazard only materializes on a manual/external hit coinciding with a network drop. Real, worth fixing per the suggested one-liner, but honest severity is P2 debt, not P1 latent-serious.

confirmed.title: Frontend Stop-Backtest button calls POST /api/backtest/stop which does not exist; no server-side cancel for a running backtest at all
confirmed.claim: api.ts defines stopBacktest() -> POST /api/backtest/stop, and the backtest page's Stop button dispatches it, but backend/api/backtest.py has no /stop route (grep finds only @router.post("/optimize/stop") at :305) — the call 404s, the catch{} swallows it, the button appears to work, and a multi-minute walk-forward backtest genuinely cannot be cancelled because _run_backtest_async has no stop-flag check either.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T5/API9 (inventory: dead/undocumented routes) + role-charter item 'undocumented breaking drift vs frontend/src/lib/api.ts callers'; apiFetch 404 branch (api.ts:131-132) proves the throw path the catch{} hides.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add POST /api/backtest/stop that sets a cancel flag consumed between walk-forward windows in _run_backtest_async (mirroring the optimizer's status-flag stop), or remove stopBacktest() and disable the Stop button for plain backtests; also drop stopBacktest() from handleStopOptimizer's Promise.allSettled.
confirmed.verify_reason: Confirmed. api.ts:497-498 defines stopBacktest() -> POST /api/backtest/stop, but backend/api/backtest.py's route table has no /stop (only /optimize/stop at :305), so the call 404s; apiFetch throws on 404 (api.ts:131-132) and both callers swallow it (page.tsx:560 allSettled, :569-573 catch{}), making the Stop button (page.tsx:675, tooltip promises 'Stop backtest after current window') a silent no-op. _run_backtest_async (backtest.py:880) runs engine.run_backtest in one run_in_executor call with no stop/cancel parameter — the stop_check lambda at :1082 is optimizer-only — so a running walk-forward backtest genuinely cannot be cancelled server-side. No fix present, no CLAUDE.md carve-out, and no matching item in phase 69-73 registers (only a phase-44 endpoint inventory mention, not a defect entry). P2 is honest: dead endpoint + missing cancellation is debt/UX deception, not live correctness or security.

confirmed.title: POST /api/backtest/ingest calls sync screen_universe() (yfinance universe download) inline in async def and holds the request open for the whole ingestion
confirmed.claim: run_data_ingestion calls screen_universe() — a sync yfinance screen over the S&P-500 universe with 6mo price history — directly on the event loop (not to_thread), then awaits the full multi-minute run_full_ingestion inline and only then returns 'completed'; the frontend's 30s AbortController (api.ts:80) always times out first, so the operator sees a timeout error while ingestion silently continues with no job id or pollable status for THIS run.
confirmed.evidence:
backtest.py:184 `async def run_data_ingestion(req: IngestRequest = IngestRequest(start_date=None, end_date=None)):`; :195-196 `screen = screen_universe()
        tickers = [s["ticker"] for s in screen] if screen else []`; :200-202 `result = await asyncio.to_thread(
            service.run_full_ingestion,
            tickers=tickers,`; :207 `return {"status": "completed", "result": result}`. screener.py:91-104 `def screen_universe(` ... `period: str = "6mo"` ... 'Cost: $0 (yfinance only...)'.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T1 rules 1 and 4 (blocking I/O in async def; >1s work does not belong in request scope) + https://github.com/zhanymkanov/fastapi-best-practices (BackgroundTasks/queue for long work); role charter 'endpoints doing heavy sync work inline instead of background tasks'; contrast the compliant POST /run pattern (backtest.py:134 returns run_id immediately).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Mirror POST /run: wrap the whole ingestion (screen included) in a background task with an _ingest_state status dict, return 202 {status:'started'} immediately, and have /ingest/status report per-run progress; minimally, move screen_universe() inside the to_thread call.
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified in code: backtest.py:195 calls sync screen_universe() (screener.py:91 — get_sp500_tickers Wikipedia scrape + yf.download of ~500 tickers, 6mo) directly on the event loop inside async def, violating the project's own .claude/rules/backend-api.md async-safety rule; the endpoint then awaits the full multi-minute run_full_ingestion inline and only returns 'completed' at line 207, with no run_id/background task (contrast POST /run at :111-135). Frontend confirmed: api.ts:80 sets a 30s AbortController on every apiFetch and runDataIngestion (api.ts:513-519) passes no custom signal, so the request always aborts client-side while ingestion continues server-side; GET /ingest/status (:213-226) returns only table row counts, not per-run progress. No neutralizing wrapper, not fixed, and no matching item in confirmed_findings.json or audit_phase69 registers. Severity honest at P2: operator-triggered admin path, transient event-loop block plus guaranteed timeout UX — debt, not live-money correctness. Note: per backend-api.md the fix should use loop.run_in_executor(_heavy_executor, ...) rather than asyncio.to_thread for the background ingestion.

confirmed.title: Portfolio routes make N sequential sync yfinance network calls inside async def (per-position event-loop blocking)
confirmed.claim: list_positions, add_position, and get_portfolio_performance (all async def, all live frontend paths via api.ts:204-227) call _enrich_position which performs a synchronous yfinance_tool.get_comprehensive_financials network fetch per position directly on the event loop — a portfolio of N positions blocks all backend traffic for N sequential yfinance round-trips (seconds each; worse when yfinance rate-limits).
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T1 rule 1 (async def calling yf.* = event-loop blocking) + https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/async/; .claude/rules/backend-api.md §Async safety names yfinance explicitly. Charts.py:23 shows the in-repo compliant pattern (`await asyncio.to_thread(yfinance_tool.get_price_history, ...)`).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Either declare these routes plain def (FastAPI threadpools them) or enrich via await asyncio.to_thread with a bounded gather across positions; reuse the live_price_cache the paper-trading router already has.
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified in code: portfolio.py:54-61, :94-135 (async def routes looping per-position) and :152-168 (_enrich_position calling yfinance_tool.get_comprehensive_financials at :156) match the quoted evidence exactly; the file contains no await/asyncio/to_thread. get_comprehensive_financials (yfinance_tool.py:12-16) is a raw uncached yf.Ticker(ticker).info blocking HTTP fetch. Live path: router mounted main.py:495, frontend calls all three routes (api.ts:205/215/226). Directly violates .claude/rules/backend-api.md §Async safety, which names yfinance explicitly; charts.py:23 shows the in-repo compliant asyncio.to_thread pattern. No phase-69..73 register duplicate (existing portfolio findings target portfolio_manager.py, different defects). P2 honest: live event-loop-blocking performance debt, but no data corruption or security impact and small current portfolios bound the damage.

confirmed.title: async def get_optimizer_status (a polled endpoint) runs pgrep subprocesses and full TSV file reads inline on the event loop
confirmed.claim: get_optimizer_status is async def with zero awaits: it loops up to three subprocess.run(["pgrep", ...]) fork+execs and reads/parses quant_results.tsv (plus optimizer_best.json) synchronously on the event loop; it is a frontend polling endpoint (QuietAccessFilter class), so every poll tick imposes fork+file-scan latency on all concurrent requests, growing with the TSV.
confirmed.evidence:
backtest.py:351-354 `@router.get("/optimize/status")
async def get_optimizer_status():
    """Current optimizer state. Also checks for CLI-launched optimizer."""
    import subprocess`; :362-364 `for pattern in optimizer_patterns:
            result = subprocess.run(["pgrep", "-f", pattern], capture_output=True, text=True)
            if result.stdout.strip():`; :374-376 `if os.path.exists(tsv_path):
                with open(tsv_path) as f:
                    lines = f.readlines()`.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T1 rule 1; .claude/rules/backend-api.md §Async safety: 'For endpoints that are purely sync (e.g., TSV file reads with no async calls), use def instead of async def'. Also note :375 open() without encoding="utf-8" violates the same rule file's Encoding convention.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Change to plain def (threadpool) since the body is purely sync, or wrap the pgrep+file section in await asyncio.to_thread; cache the pgrep probe with a short TTL like cron_dashboard's _launchctl_state.
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified in code: get_optimizer_status (backtest.py:352) is async def with zero awaits, runs up to 3 subprocess.run(["pgrep","-f",...]) fork+execs (line 363) plus full readlines() of quant_results.tsv (375-376, 408-409) and optimizer_best.json (391) inline on the event loop. Frontend polls it every 2s (frontend/src/app/backtest/page.tsx:488 setInterval(refreshStatus, 2000) -> getOptimizerStatus -> /api/backtest/optimize/status, api.ts:538). Violates .claude/rules/backend-api.md Async safety rule (use plain def for purely-sync endpoints; sibling /optimize/runs at :419 is correctly plain def) and the encoding="utf-8" convention (lines 375/391/408). Not a duplicate: phase-69 register's only async-safety item targets orchestrator.py yfinance, not this endpoint. Minor evidence drift: the route is NOT actually in QuietAccessFilter._QUIET_PREFIXES (main.py lists /api/optimizer/status, not /api/backtest/optimize/status), but frontend 2s polling independently proves the polled-endpoint premise. P2 honest: latency/debt, not correctness.

confirmed.title: POST /api/skills/optimize runs a potentially infinite sync optimization loop via BackgroundTasks, permanently occupying a finite threadpool slot
confirmed.claim: start_optimization schedules the sync _run_optimization_loop through Starlette BackgroundTasks with max_iterations=0 meaning 'unlimited'; sync background tasks execute in the shared anyio threadpool (default ~40 tokens), so an unbounded LLM-driven optimizer run consumes a slot for hours/forever, starving plain-def routes and asyncio.to_thread calls backend-wide, and its failures are silent (BackgroundTasks has no status surface — unlike the sibling backtest/optimizer state dicts). max_iterations is also an unvalidated query int (negatives accepted).
confirmed.evidence: skills.py:36 `def _run_optimization_loop(max_iterations: int = 0):`; :47 `optimizer.run_loop(max_iterations=max_iterations, target_agents=target_agents)`; :52-53 `@router.post("/optimize")\nasync def start_optimization(background_tasks: BackgroundTasks, max_iterations: int = 0):`; :59-60 `background_tasks.add_task(_run_optimization_loop, max_iterations)\n    return {"status": "started", "max_iterations": max_iterations or "unlimited"}`.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T1 rule 4 ('BackgroundTasks usages: >1s or must-not-fail work belongs in Celery') + https://github.com/zhanymkanov/fastapi-best-practices (BackgroundTasks sub-second rule; finite threadpool); T1 rule 2 (starving the threadpool stalls ALL sync routes).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Replace BackgroundTasks with the backtest.py pattern: a module-level asyncio task wrapping asyncio.to_thread on a dedicated executor (or a subprocess), with a status dict; add Query(ge=0, le=...) on max_iterations.
confirmed.verify_reason: All quoted evidence verified at skills.py:36/47/52-53/59-60. run_loop (skill_optimizer.py:661) is an unbounded sync while-loop when max_iterations<=0 (endpoint default 0; negatives also pass the >0 check), executed via Starlette BackgroundTasks -> run_in_threadpool, permanently occupying a shared anyio threadpool token. Aggravating context: the is_running 409 guard is racy (_running set only after establish_baseline runs inside the background task), so rapid POSTs can enqueue multiple infinite loops; get_status() (line 860) has no error/traceback field, so a baseline crash (phase-69 confirmed a real one) dies with only a log line. This violates the project's own .claude/rules/backend-api.md convention (_heavy_executor + error/traceback state dicts, per backtest.py) and research brief 75.0 T1 rule 4. Not fixed, not intentional, no duplicate in phase-69/70-73 registers (the only skill_optimizer register item is the distinct outcome_tracker datetime crash). The 'starves routes backend-wide' phrasing overstates a single token, but the core defect stands; P2 is honest for operator-triggered debt.

confirmed.title: GET /api/logs/tail scans entire log files (38+ MB) and GET /api/jobs/all forks launchctl subprocesses inside async def routes
confirmed.claim: get_log_tail is async def and computes the tail via deque(f, maxlen=n), which synchronously reads the WHOLE file through the event loop — the file map's own comment says mas-harness.log is '38+ MB, growing', so each /cron page load blocks the backend for a full multi-MB scan; get_all_jobs (also async def) calls _launchctl_state per launchd entry, which runs subprocess.run inline (cached only 30s, so the first hit each window forks several launchctl processes on the loop).
confirmed.evidence:
cron_dashboard_api.py:530-531 `@router.get("/logs/tail")
async def get_log_tail(`; :556-558 `try:
        with p.open(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace") as f:
            tail = list(deque(f, maxlen=n))`; :153 comment `# ... Live file is 38+ MB, growing.`; :410-411 `@router.get("/jobs/all")
async def get_all_jobs()`; :455 `probe = _launchctl_state(entry["id"])` with :267 `proc = subprocess.run(` inside it.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T1 rule 1; .claude/rules/backend-api.md §Async safety ('file reads >100ms' named explicitly). Secondary: Query le=10000 at :533 vs silent clamp to _LINES_MAX=1000 at :543/:169 advertises a contract the handler doesn't honor.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Wrap the tail read + launchctl probes in await asyncio.to_thread; replace the full-file deque scan with a seek-from-end block reader; align the lines Query upper bound with _LINES_MAX (le=1000).
confirmed.verify_reason: All quoted evidence verified at the claimed lines: async def get_log_tail (:530-531) reads the whole file via deque(f, maxlen=n) (:556-558) with no to_thread/seek-from-end, and async def get_all_jobs (:410-411) calls _launchctl_state per _LAUNCHD_JOBS entry (:454-455, 6 entries) which runs subprocess.run with a 5s timeout inline on cache miss (:267-271, 30s TTL) — worst case ~30s of loop blockage. The scale claim is understated: the allowlisted repo-root backend.log is 86 MB on disk right now (mas-harness.log absent, but backend.log alone proves multi-MB synchronous scans). Directly violates .claude/rules/backend-api.md §Async safety, which names file reads >100ms and mandates asyncio.to_thread or sync def. Secondary Query le=10000 vs _LINES_MAX=1000 silent clamp (:533 vs :543/:169) also verified. No neutralizing wrapper; not in any phase-69/70-73 register (phase-69's blocking-I/O item is orchestrator.py:1810, a different file/defect). P2 honest: dashboard-triggered event-loop stalls that hang concurrent requests, but not live trading correctness or security.

confirmed.title: Fire-and-forget asyncio.create_task without saved references for backtest, analysis, and paper-trading cycle runs
confirmed.claim: The three flagship long-running operations are launched with bare asyncio.create_task(...) and the returned Task is discarded; the event loop keeps only weak references to tasks, so per the official asyncio docs a mid-flight backtest, analysis, or manually-triggered trading cycle can be garbage-collected and vanish silently, leaving _backtest_state/_tasks stuck at 'running' forever with no error (the optimizer path at :299 correctly saves _optimizer_task, proving the intended pattern).
confirmed.evidence:
backtest.py:134 `asyncio.create_task(_run_backtest_async(run_id, req))` (return value dropped); analysis.py:363-364 `# Launch as a fire-and-forget asyncio task
        asyncio.create_task(_run_sync_analysis(task_id, ticker, settings))`; paper_trading.py:1034 `asyncio.create_task(_run_cycle_background(settings))`; contrast backtest.py:299-301 `_optimizer_task = asyncio.create_task(
        _run_optimizer_async(req.max_iterations, req.use_llm)
    )`.
confirmed.basis: Official asyncio doc: https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-task.html#asyncio.create_task — 'Important: Save a reference to the result of this function... The event loop only keeps weak references to tasks. A task that isn't referenced elsewhere may get garbage collected at any time'; research_brief_75.0.md T2 (Python 3.14 asyncio correctness sweep).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Store each Task in a module-level var or a module-level set with task.add_done_callback(set.discard); add a done-callback that flips the state dict to 'error' if the task ends with an exception.
confirmed.verify_reason: All four quoted sites verified exactly: bare create_task at backtest.py:134, analysis.py:364 (with the 'fire-and-forget' comment at :363), paper_trading.py:1034; saved-reference counter-example _optimizer_task at backtest.py:299. No wrapper/guard neutralizes it — the coroutines catch their own exceptions but nothing prevents task GC, and a collected task would leave _backtest_state/_tasks stuck at 'running' with the 409 mutual-exclusion guard then permanently blocking new runs. No duplicate in phase-69/70/71/72/73 registers (grep across handoff/current/audit_phase69, confirmed_findings.json, handoff/audit). Severity note: pending tasks awaiting executor futures are in practice strongly referenced via the future callback chain in CPython, so real-world GC is rare — that caps this below P1 — but it violates the explicit asyncio doc contract (RUF006 class), affects all three flagship long-running operations, and the trivial fix matches the codebase's own _optimizer_task pattern. P2 (debt) is honest; keep as-is.

confirmed.title: Systemic response_model/status_code gap: 118 of ~139 routes return raw dicts with undeclared schemas
confirmed.claim: Only 21 of ~139 routes declare response_model and only one success status_code is declared repo-wide (portfolio POST 201); every other route returns hand-built dicts with no schema, so OpenAPI is blind, field drift is undetectable at build time, and OWASP API3-style overexposure filtering never applies — this exact class already bit twice (phase-69: dead cockpit kill-switch tile reading a never-returned max_drawdown_pct; Slack /portfolio formatter reading non-existent keys), and the shape-drifting GET /status here is representative.
confirmed.evidence: grep -c 'response_model' backend/api/*.py totals 21 across 24 files vs ~139 @router.* decorators (route census 2026-07-19); paper_trading.py:116-117 `@router.get("/status")\nasync def get_status():` returns the hand-built dict at :147-166 incl. ad-hoc alias `"last_run_ts": loop_status.get("last_run")` (:165) — no model anywhere; contrast the fully modeled sovereign_api.py:319-698 (RedLineResponse etc.).
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T1 rule 7 ('every route declares response_model + status_code', zhanymkanov canon) + T5/API3 (OWASP: 'pydantic response_model prevents overexposure — audit raw-dict returns', https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0x11-t10/); phase-69 register lines 66 and 72 prove the consequence class (annotation, not re-find).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Adopt a per-router incremental policy: define pydantic response models for the frontend-consumed money-path routers first (paper_trading, backtest status/results), generated to match frontend/src/lib/types.ts, and add response_model= + explicit status_code= per route; enforce via a lint/test that counts undeclared routes.
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified: exactly 21 response_model occurrences across backend/api/*.py vs 130 @router.(get|post|put|delete|patch) decorators (finding's ~139 is minor census drift), and exactly one explicit success status_code repo-wide (portfolio.py:64 status_code=201). paper_trading.py:116-117 GET /status returns the hand-built dict at 147-166 with the ad-hoc last_run_ts alias at 165 and an alternate not_initialized shape at 131 — no model, matching the claim. Return-type annotations do not neutralize it (only cost_budget_api, already modeled, annotates pydantic returns; others are -> Dict/untyped, yielding no OpenAPI schema). Not fixed, and contrary to being intentional, .claude/rules/backend-api.md itself mandates pydantic response models. Not queued: harness_log.md:15417 is a one-route deferred-hardening note, and phase-69 register items 66/72 are the consequence bugs the finding cites as annotation, not this systemic gap. P3 honest: systemic debt/polish, no direct live defect.

confirmed.title: mas_events mutation + ingest endpoints return validation errors as 200 {"error": ...} with unvalidated raw-dict bodies
confirmed.claim: POST /api/mas/agents/{agent_type}/model takes body: dict (no pydantic model) and returns {"error": "Unknown agent..."} and {"error": "model is required"} with HTTP 200 instead of 404/422, and POST /events/ingest returns {"ok": false, "error": str(e)} with 200 on failure — callers (Slack App Home plumbing) cannot detect failure by status, and this error envelope ({error} @200) contradicts the {detail} + 4xx/5xx envelope every other router uses.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T1 ('excessively use Pydantic'; response/status discipline) + role-charter 'inconsistent error envelope shapes across routers'; FastAPI convention: raise HTTPException(404/422) so the documented {detail} envelope + status semantics hold (https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/handling-errors/).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add a pydantic body model ({model: str} with min_length=1), raise HTTPException(404) for unknown agent and let pydantic 422 missing model; make ingest return 400 on malformed events; align both to the {detail} envelope.
confirmed.verify_reason: Evidence verified verbatim: update_agent_model takes raw dict body and returns {"error": ...} at HTTP 200 for unknown agent (lines 212-213) and missing model (215-217); /events/ingest returns {"ok": False, "error": str(e)} at 200 (83-85). No HTTPException or status_code anywhere in the file. In context nothing neutralizes it: the only automated caller (_forward_remote in backend/agents/mas_events.py:132-144) is fire-and-forget and cannot detect failure; the model endpoint is operator/Slack-facing with no status semantics. Violates the project's own .claude/rules/backend-api.md ("Use Pydantic models for request/response validation", "Input validation on all endpoints") and diverges from the {detail}+4xx envelope other routers use. No duplicate in phase-69/70/71/72/73 registers (no mention of mas_events, update_agent_model, or events/ingest). Observability-plumbing scope, so P3 is the honest severity.

confirmed.title: POST /api/harness/monthly-approval/{month_key} lacks the month_key pattern its own GET has, and answers invalid actions with 200 status="rejected"
confirmed.claim: The read-only GET validates month_key with pattern ^\d{4}-\d{2}$ but the MUTATING POST accepts any string as month_key, and an invalid action value returns HTTP 200 with a MonthlyApprovalState whose status field is "rejected" — the same vocabulary as a genuinely recorded rejection — so a caller with a typo'd action can believe the month's champion-challenger promotion was actively rejected when nothing was recorded (reason string is the only tell); this route is also on the unauthenticated /api/harness/monthly-approval public prefix, raising the cost of lax input handling.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T1 (path/query param validation via pydantic; charter 'wrong/missing status codes... for validation') + T5/API2 context (unauthenticated business-flow surface); FastAPI path-param validation doc (https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/path-params-numeric-validations/).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add Path(pattern=r"^\d{4}-\d{2}$") on the POST month_key; make ApprovalActionBody.action a Literal["approved","rejected"] so bad actions 422 instead of 200-"rejected".
confirmed.verify_reason: Evidence verified exactly: GET validates month_key with Query pattern ^\d{4}-\d{2}$ (line 169) while the mutating POST (line 185) takes an unconstrained str, and ApprovalActionBody.action is a plain str so an invalid action returns HTTP 200 with status="rejected" (lines 187-193) — the same terminal vocabulary as a real recorded rejection, distinguishable only via the reason string. The route is unauthenticated (main.py:412 _PUBLIC_PATHS). No neutralizer: record_approval does not validate month_key format either (bogus keys harmlessly return the no_row_to_resolve pending response, which caps severity), and the "degrade to rejected" comment shows the 200-rejected path is deliberate but it contradicts the project rule requiring Pydantic input validation on all endpoints. Not in the phase-69 register (the only monthly_champion_challenger item there concerns the BQ activation flip at :269, a different defect). No state corruption or security escalation, so P3 polish is the honest severity.

confirmed.title: reports list endpoints take unbounded bare-int limit params (negative -> BQ error surfaced as 502; huge -> unbounded payload)
confirmed.claim: list_reports and get_cost_history declare `limit: int` with no ge/le bounds (unlike paper_trading's Query(100, ge=1, le=1000) convention); limit=-1 flows into the parameterized `LIMIT @limit` and BigQuery rejects it, which the handler maps to a 502/500 server-error class for what is a client validation error, and limit=10_000_000 returns the entire deduped report table in one response — both also pollute the response cache keyed on the raw limit.
confirmed.evidence:
reports.py:29 `async def list_reports(limit: int = 20, bq: BigQueryClient = Depends(_get_bq)):`; :88 `async def get_cost_history(limit: int = 50, bq: BigQueryClient = Depends(_get_bq)):`; sink: bigquery_client.py:276-279 `LIMIT @limit
        """
        job_config = bigquery.QueryJobConfig(
            query_parameters=[bigquery.ScalarQueryParameter("limit", "INT64", limit)]` (error path reports.py:41-46 maps GoogleAPIError to 502). Contrast paper_trading.py:246 `limit: int = Query(100, ge=1, le=1000)`.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T5/API4 (Unrestricted Resource Consumption) + T1 pydantic validation discipline; charter items 'path/query param validation gaps' and 'missing pagination on list endpoints that grow'; https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/query-params-str-validations/.
confirmed.suggested_fix: limit: int = Query(20, ge=1, le=500) on both routes (and the same for any other bare-int limits found by grep 'limit: int =' across backend/api/).
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified in code: reports.py:29 (limit: int = 20) and :88 (limit: int = 50) are bare unbounded ints; both flow unclamped into parameterized LIMIT @limit in bigquery_client.py get_recent_reports (:276) and get_cost_history (:369). Negative limit yields a BigQuery error mapped to 502 (reports.py:41-46, 99-101) instead of a 422 client error; huge limit is passed through unchecked; cache keys at :33/:92 embed the raw limit. The repo's own convention (paper_trading.py:246 Query(100, ge=1, le=1000); :279 Query(365, ge=1, le=3650)) and security.md's 'All API endpoints validate input parameters' rule show this is a genuine gap, not intentional. No duplicate in confirmed_findings.json, audit_phase69, design_pack_73, or harness_proposals.json. Severity P3 is honest: endpoints are behind the auth email-whitelist middleware, the query stays parameterized (no injection), and the table is small — impact is wrong status-code class, oversized-but-finite payload, and minor cache-key proliferation. Suggested fix (bounded Query) matches house style.

confirmed.title: GET /api/backtest/runs re-reads the whole experiments TSV per BASELINE row (O(N^2)) and has no pagination on an unboundedly growing dataset
confirmed.claim: list_backtest_runs opens quant_results.tsv once, then for EVERY baseline row re-opens and re-scans the entire file to count children — quadratic in file length — and returns all rows with no limit/offset; the TSV grows by one row per optimizer experiment (max_iterations=0 'run forever' mode) so both cost and payload grow without bound (521 rows today), and each has_detail fallback additionally loads full result JSONs.
confirmed.evidence:
backtest.py:666-676 `if is_baseline:
                # Count children in the same TSV that have this run as parent
                with open(tsv_path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as exp_file:
                    exp_header = exp_file.readline().strip().split("\t")
                    child_count = 0
                    for exp_line in exp_file:` ... `if exp_row.get("parent_run_id") == rid:
                            child_count += 1`; :714 `return {"runs": rows}` (no pagination params on :603-604 `@router.get("/runs")
def list_backtest_runs():`). wc -l quant_results.tsv = 521 (2026-07-19).
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T6 anchor #7 (Anthropic writing-tools-for-agents: 'pagination, range selection, filtering, and/or truncation with sensible default parameter values' — same principle for HTTP list endpoints) + charter 'missing pagination on list endpoints that grow'; T1 rule 2 (heavy sync work in def routes burns the finite threadpool).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Build a parent_run_id->count map in the single first pass (dict counter) instead of nested rescans, and add limit/offset Query params (default limit=100) to /runs and /optimize/experiments.
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified verbatim: list_backtest_runs (backtest.py:603-714) re-opens and fully re-scans quant_results.tsv inside the row loop for every BASELINE row (line 668) to count children, and returns all rows with no limit/offset params. TSV measured at 521 rows / 26 baselines, growing one row per optimizer experiment. No cache, guard, or wrapper neutralizes it and no phase-69/70/71/72/73 register item covers it. Mitigations keep it P3: the route is sync `def` (runs in FastAPI threadpool per project rules, event loop unaffected) and current cost is milliseconds — it is real but low-urgency debt; the single-pass counter-dict + pagination fix is trivial.

confirmed.title: GET /api/backtest/results raises HTTPException(202) — an error-shaped {detail} body on a success status code
confirmed.claim: When a backtest is running with no prior result, the route raises HTTPException(202, 'Backtest still running'), producing status 202 with body {"detail": ...}; every HTTP client treats 2xx as success (frontend apiFetch checks res.ok, so 202 passes and res.json() would be returned typed as BacktestResults), so the 'still running' signal is indistinguishable from a malformed result — currently masked only because page.tsx:425 guards on status.has_result before fetching.
confirmed.evidence: backtest.py:162-164 `if _backtest_state["status"] == "running":\n        raise HTTPException(202, "Backtest still running")\n    raise HTTPException(404, "No backtest results available")`; api.ts:110 `if (!res.ok) {` (202 is ok=true, so the error branches at :125-134 never run) then :136 `return res.json();`.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T1 rule 7 (explicit status_code discipline); FastAPI handling-errors doc — HTTPException is the error-return mechanism producing the {detail} envelope (https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/handling-errors/): using it for a 2xx yields a success status with an error body, breaking the envelope contract.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Return JSONResponse(status_code=202, content={"status": "running", "result": None}) with a shape the frontend type can discriminate, or keep 404 semantics for 'no results yet' and let /status carry running-ness (it already does via has_result).
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified: backtest.py:163 raises HTTPException(202, "Backtest still running"), producing status 202 with FastAPI's error envelope {"detail": ...}. apiFetch (frontend/src/lib/api.ts:110) treats 202 as ok and returns the error body via res.json() typed as BacktestResults; the only thing preventing this live is the has_result guard at frontend/src/app/backtest/page.tsx:425, which happens to be false in exactly the state that triggers the 202 (first run in progress, no stash). The 2xx-with-error-body contract violation is real for any other client (curl, tests, MCP). Not blessed by .claude/rules/backend-api.md conventions, not present in phase-69-73 registers. P3 is honest: latent polish, currently masked, one-line fix (drop the 202 branch in favor of 404 + /status has_result, or return a discriminable JSONResponse).

confirmed.title: MCP_ARCHITECTURE.md: BigQuery MCP 'not pinned in .mcp.json' is false; slack MCP row is phantom
confirmed.claim: The MCP reference doc (pointed to by ARCHITECTURE.md as the detailed reference) asserts the BigQuery MCP server is harness-injected and not pinned in .mcp.json, and lists a slack MCP server at @latest as a registered external server. docs/MCP_ARCHITECTURE.md:30: "The BigQuery MCP server is **harness-injected**, not pinned in `.mcp.json`."
docs/MCP_ARCHITECTURE.md:25: "| `slack` (@anthropic-ai/mcp-server-slack) | stdio (npx) | `@latest` (Anthropic-maintained) |"
.mcp.json:17-18: "--from", "mcp-server-bigquery==0.3.2", "mcp-server-bigquery",
grep -n slack .mcp.json -> no matches
CLAUDE.md:113-114: "The BigQuery MCP server is **pinned in `.mcp.json`** as of phase-23.2.21. Package: `mcp-server-bigquery==0.3.2`"

confirmed.suggested_fix: Update the external-server table to the actual .mcp.json roster (alpaca 2.0.1, bigquery 0.3.2, paper-search-mcp 0.1.3, playwright 0.0.76) and delete or correct the harness-injected BigQuery paragraph to match CLAUDE.md's pinned description.

confirmed.title: backend-slack-bot.md instructs Docker-network no-auth API calls that don't match deployment or auth reality
confirmed.claim: The slack-bot rule file (auto-loaded for backend/slack_bot edits) instructs internal API calls via 'httpx with 30s timeout to http://backend:8000. No auth tokens (internal Docker network)'. Reality: deployment is a single Mac; the live modules call http://localhost:8000 / 127.0.0.1:8000 (commands.py:22, direct_responder.py, scheduler.py:95), the Docker hostname survives only as a vestigial constant (scheduler.py:79), and the backend enforces Bearer auth on everything outside _PUBLIC_PATHS — so a developer following the rule writes calls to a dead hostname and omits auth on endpoints (e.g. /api/paper-trading/*) that require it.

.claude/rules/backend-slack-bot.md:26: "- **Internal API calls**: httpx with 30s timeout to `http://backend:8000`. No auth tokens (internal Docker network)."
backend/slack_bot/commands.py:22: _BACKEND_URL = "http://localhost:8000"
backend/slack_bot/scheduler.py:79: _BACKEND_URL = "http://backend:8000"
backend/slack_bot/scheduler.py:95: _LOCAL_BACKEND_URL = "http://127.0.0.1:8000"
backend/main.py:435: if request.method != "OPTIONS" and not any(path.startswith(p) for p in _PUBLIC_PATHS):
("/api/paper-trading" is not in _PUBLIC_PATHS, main.py:406-423)
confirmed.basis: handoff/current/research_brief_75.0.md internal recon (local-only single-Mac deployment; _PUBLIC_PATHS ground truth) + A9 charter. Whether the bot's live unauthenticated calls actually 401 is A7/slack-bot code territory; the doc instruction itself is the drift.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Rewrite the bullet to the real topology: localhost:8000 base URL, state how auth is satisfied for non-public endpoints (or that only public endpoints may be called without a token), and drop the Docker-network rationale.
confirmed.verify_reason: Line 26 quote verbatim (line 11 repeats the backend:8000 claim). Live code contradicts both halves: commands.py:22 uses localhost:8000, scheduler.py:79 backend:8000 constant is explicitly commented dead (line 73), live scheduler calls use 127.0.0.1:8000 (line 95); backend auth middleware (main.py:435) enforces get_current_user on all non-_PUBLIC_PATHS routes, /api/paper-trading//api/analysis//api/reports are not public (main.py:406-423), and auth.py:191-192 401s tokenless requests unless explicit dev bypass env flags are set — 'internal Docker network' rationale is false. Rule file auto-loads on backend/slack_bot/** edits, so it actively steers new code to a dead hostname without auth. Not in any phase-69..73 register; sibling rules (security.md, backend-api.md) confirm Bearer auth is the real convention. P2 (doc debt, no direct runtime defect) is honest.
