Agents that jump to these anchors read the wrong code and can 'verify' claims against unrelated lines.
confirmed.evidence:
CLAUDE.md:131: "per `backend/db/bigquery_client.py:486` (`_pt_table()` uses `settings.bq_dataset_reports = \"financial_reports\"`)"
grep -n _pt_table backend/db/bigquery_client.py -> 516: def _pt_table(self, name: str) -> str:
CLAUDE.md:97: "Layer 1: 15-step pipeline (1477 lines)" / wc -l -> 2308 backend/agents/orchestrator.py
CLAUDE.md:103: "Backtest engine (1167 lines)" / wc -l -> 1308 backend/backtest/backtest_engine.py
ARCHITECTURE.md:144: "`orchestrator.py:1014, 1443, 1530-1532` skips deep_dive, debate," / grep lite_mode -> orchestrator.py:1615
confirmed.basis: handoff/current/research_brief_75.0.md internal recon (module sizes wc -l 2026-07-19: orchestrator 2308, backtest_engine 1308) + A9 charter (claims naming file/function/line that no longer match).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Re-resolve each anchor (drop raw line counts entirely — cite symbols instead of line numbers where possible, e.g. 'bigquery_client.py::_pt_table'), in the same pass as the docs-08 refresh.
confirmed.verify_reason: Every cited anchor is verifiably stale. (1) CLAUDE.md:131 cites bigquery_client.py:486 for _pt_table(), but grep shows `def _pt_table` at line 516; lines 480-492 are the body of save_agent_memory, so an agent jumping to :486 reads unrelated memory-persistence code. (2) CLAUDE.md:97 and ARCHITECTURE.md:61/:413 all say orchestrator.py is 1477 lines; wc -l gives 2308. (3) CLAUDE.md:103 says backtest_engine.py is 1167 lines; actual 1308. (4) ARCHITECTURE.md:131 cites signal_attribution.py:57-157 for the 5-category grouping, but line 57 region is the _clamp01 weight-clamping helper (phase-25.D) and the Analyst-layer extraction only begins around ~155, so the range mostly covers helpers. (5) ARCHITECTURE.md:144 cites orchestrator.py:1014,1443,1530-1532 for lite-mode skips, but those lines now hold enrichment-batch cost-tracker warning handling, advisor-tool fallback comments, and critic-verdict processing respectively; the lite_mode read is at orchestrator.py:1615. The failure scenario is real for this doctrine-driven repo: agents are instructed to jump to file:line anchors and can 'verify' claims against wrong code. Not neutralized by anything in context, not already fixed, and no phase-69/70/71/72/73 register item covers stale doc anchors (phase-69 registers contain no such entry; the docs-08 reference in suggested_fix is a sibling finding in the same 75.0 audit, complementary not duplicative). P3 is the honest severity — pure documentation polish with no runtime impact.

confirmed.title: Path-scoped rules files carry stale inventory counts (routers, tools, routes)
confirmed.claim: Auto-injected rules files understate the surfaces they govern: backend-api.md says '70+ routes across 10 routers' while main.py mounts 20 routers (24 modules in backend/api/); backend-tools.md's 'Tool Registry (16 tools)' omits price_quality.py (17 tool modules on disk); frontend.md says 'src/app/ (10 routes)' vs 14 live page routes. Coverage decisions scoped by these counts (e.g. 'audited all 16 tools') silently miss the undocumented members.
confirmed.evidence:
.claude/rules/backend-api.md:16: "## API Structure (70+ routes across 10 routers)"
grep -c include_router backend/main.py -> 20
.claude/rules/backend-tools.md:14: "## Tool Registry (16 tools)"
ls backend/tools/*.py -> 18 files (17 tools + __init__.py); price_quality.py absent from the registry table
.claude/rules/frontend.md:14: "- Pages in `src/app/` (10 routes), components in `src/components/`..."
ls frontend/src/app/ -> 14 page routes (agent-map, agents, backtest, cron, learnings, login, observability, paper-trading, performance, reports, settings, signals, sovereign, /)
confirmed.basis: handoff/current/research_brief_75.0.md internal recon (20 include_router calls) + A9 charter; counts verified on disk this session.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Update the three counts and add price_quality.py to the backend-tools.md registry (source + rate-limit row); consider dropping hardcoded counts in favor of 'see directory listing' phrasing so they cannot rot.
confirmed.verify_reason: All three stale counts verified on disk: backend-api.md:16 says '10 routers' while backend/main.py has 20 include_router calls (22 router modules in backend/api/); backend-tools.md:14 says '16 tools' and its table lists 16 rows while backend/tools/ has 17 tool modules, with price_quality.py absent from all .claude/rules files (grep empty); frontend.md:14 says '10 routes' while frontend/src/app/ has 14 page routes (13 named dirs + root page.tsx). These are path-scoped auto-injected rules, so coverage decisions keyed to the counts silently miss undocumented members. Only trivial evidence drift: '24 modules' counts __init__.py and __pycache__; real router-module count is 22, which does not weaken the claim. Not fixed, not intentional, and no duplicate in phase-69-73 registers (confirmed_findings.json, audit_phase69/contested.json, harness_proposals.json all checked). P3 is the honest severity: documentation rot with no runtime effect.

confirmed.title: Any user's white_check_mark reaction on ANY message in #ford-approvals executes git push origin main
confirmed.claim: The live reaction_added handler pushes to origin/main on any :white_check_mark: reaction in the approvals channel: it checks neither WHO reacted (no operator allowlist) nor WHICH message was reacted to (no binding to a specific push-approval request ts). An accidental checkmark on a digest/ticket-ack message by any workspace member publishes whatever unreviewed local commits exist, bypassing the away-ops PreToolUse push guard (which only gates harness processes, not the bot). Phase-69.1 fixed the sibling sink in the same file ('clear queue' pkill, register row commands.py:295) but left this one; it also runs a sync 30s subprocess on the event loop.
confirmed.evidence:
commands.py:328-347:
    @app.event("reaction_added")
    async def handle_reaction(event, say):
        """Handle ✅/❌ reactions on push approval messages."""
        reaction = event.get("reaction", "")
        item = event.get("item", {})
        channel = item.get("channel", "")
        if channel != _APPROVAL_CHANNEL:
            return
        if reaction == "white_check_mark":
            ...
                result = subprocess.check_output(
                    ["git", "push", "origin", "main"],
confirmed.basis: handoff/current/research_brief_75.0.md T5 (OWASP API Security Top 10 2023: API5 Broken Function Level Authorization; API2) and the phase-69 register precedent that workspace-message-triggered privileged sinks in this exact handler family are critical (register.md row commands.py:295, fixed 69.1). Repo's own fail-closed pattern exists at operator_tokens.py:87-95 and is not applied here. Not present in any exclusion register (grep for reaction/white_check/git push over register.md, confirmed_findings.json, harness_proposals.json: zero hits).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Gate on event['user'] == settings.slack_operator_user_id (fail-closed when unset, like is_operator_token_message); bind approval to the ts of a bot-posted push-approval request (track pending-approval message ts, require item.ts to match); run the push via await asyncio.to_thread(...).
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified in commands.py:328-355: handle_reaction gates only on channel==_APPROVAL_CHANNEL and reaction=='white_check_mark', then runs subprocess.check_output(['git','push','origin','main']) synchronously in the async handler. No event['user'] allowlist, no binding to a bot-posted approval message ts, and no middleware in app.py filters reaction events. The repo's own fail-closed operator gate (operator_tokens.py:87-95, using settings.slack_operator_user_id which exists in backend/config/settings.py) is applied to the token handler in the same file but not here; phase-69.1 fixed the sibling 'clear queue' pkill sink (comment at commands.py:293-297) and left this one. Greps for reaction/white_check/push-origin over audit_phase69/register.md, away_ops/defect_register.md, confirmed_findings.json, and harness_proposals.json show no existing register item — not a duplicate. P1 stands: live reachable privileged sink, but requires approvals-channel membership and publishes local commits rather than moving funds.

confirmed.title: P0 kill-switch iMessage pager logs 'sent' without checking imsg exit code; operator phone hardcoded in source
confirmed.claim: send_trading_escalation's L2 leg (the LAST-resort pager for 'Kill Switch Activated' P0s) runs subprocess.run without check=True and never inspects returncode/stderr, then unconditionally logs 'iMessage escalation sent'. If `imsg send` fails (Messages auth revoked, recipient error, sandbox denial) the P0 page is silently dropped and the log claims success — the exact silent-paging failure class the pending 72.0.4 step targets, but at a seam (scheduler.py send_trading_escalation) 72.0.4's anchors (autonomous_loop.py:360-398, 902-958) do not cover. The operator's personal phone number is also hardcoded in source.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T5 (OWASP Secrets Management Cheat Sheet: never hardcode; config belongs in env/settings) + T8 empirical-gate discipline (a returncode check IS the cheap empirical gate). Distinct from pending 72.0.4 (different file and seam; verified imsg exists at /opt/homebrew/bin/imsg so the path is live).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Capture proc = subprocess.run(...); on proc.returncode != 0 log ERROR with stderr and post a Slack fallback line ('P0 iMessage pager FAILED: ...') so the L1 channel records the L2 failure; move _ESCALATION_PHONE to backend.config.settings.
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified at scheduler.py:954-969: subprocess.run for the P0 iMessage leg uses capture_output without check=True, never inspects returncode/stderr, and line 967 unconditionally logs 'iMessage escalation sent' — a nonzero imsg exit (auth revoked, bad recipient, sandbox denial) is silently mislogged as success on the last-resort kill-switch pager. No caller/wrapper neutralizes it (notify_kill_switch_activated and alerting.py rely on this function directly). The same file proves the fix is the local convention: scheduler.py:1022-1029 checks proc.returncode and posts a Slack fallback. Hardcoded phone +4794810537 at line 955 violates .claude/rules/security.md (config belongs in settings/Secret Manager); note the same number is also hardcoded in queue_notification.py:34,63,164 and sla_monitor.py:20, widening the config-extraction fix. Not a duplicate: masterplan 72.0.4 is scoped to autonomous_loop.py P1 degraded-cycle alerts via the Slack bot-token path (different file, channel, and severity class; 'no new alert classes'), and no imsg/phone item exists in the phase-69..73 registers (confirmed_findings.json, harness_proposals.json, audit_phase69). P1 honest: latent-serious (only bites when imsg exits nonzero during a P0, L1 Slack usually delivers) but the success log lies on the terminal escalation channel.

confirmed.title: Deploy-from-Slack control plane (self_update.py) is unreachable: its only caller is dead code; 'deploy update' goes to the LLM
confirmed.claim: handle_deploy_command has exactly one call site — assistant_handler.handle_user_message — and no module anywhere imports assistant_handler (grep-verified across backend/, scripts/, tests). The registered assistant path is assistant_lifecycle -> streaming_integration, which contains zero deploy routing; in #ford-approvals a 'deploy update' message just becomes a ticket. So the documented remote-recovery mechanism ('Since Slack is the primary interface to the Mac Mini', self_update.py:4-5, with 'deploy update/rollback/status' commands) does not exist: typing 'deploy update' while away reaches the MAS orchestrator, which can reply as if it deployed. Silent capability loss plus an LLM-hallucination trap on the recovery path.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T8 round-6 (RepoAudit: pair neural auditors with SYMBOLIC pre-analysis — the import-graph grep is the deterministic proof) + the mandate's stated gap (self_update.py caller-authorization/wiring unread by round 1). Distinct from security-02 (filed on assistant_handler.py): this finding is the self_update.py capability being orphaned, with no alternate route (grep 'deploy' across backend/slack_bot shows no other handler).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Operator decision: (a) revive — route deploy commands in streaming_integration/commands.py BEFORE LLM classification, gated by the gap1-04 allowlist, and add a wiring regression test; or (b) remove self_update.py and its docstring/help claims, standardizing on away-ops scripts for recovery. Either way, make the assistant refuse deploy-verb messages instead of free-answering them.
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified end-to-end. handle_deploy_command is at self_update.py:436 with the quoted dispatch. Its sole caller is assistant_handler.py:238-239, and a repo-wide grep (py/sh/json/toml, excluding .venv) shows NO module imports assistant_handler — only .claude/masterplan.json mentions it historically. The live wiring is app.py:32-34 -> register_commands (commands.py: zero deploy routing, no /deploy slash command) + register_assistant_lifecycle -> assistant_lifecycle.py:151-153 -> streaming_integration.handle_user_message_with_streaming (zero 'deploy' hits). So 'deploy update' in Slack reaches the LLM path; the documented remote-recovery mechanism (self_update.py docstring lines 4-12) is unreachable. Not intentional: smoke_test_4_17_9 and the phase research brief list self_update.py as 'Active' deploy flow, and harness_log 6910 describes the deploy-check as wired — the project believes the capability exists. No duplicate in phase-69/70/71/72/73 registers (grep of handoff/current confirmed_findings.json and handoff/ found no self_update entry); distinct from security-02 which is filed on assistant_handler.py itself. P1 is honest: latent-serious silent capability loss on the away-ops recovery path plus an LLM-hallucination trap, but not a live trading-correctness/security break (not P0).

confirmed.title: Deploy plane has zero caller authorization: text-only API, historical wiring ran it for every assistant message
confirmed.claim: handle_deploy_command(text) accepts only message text — no user_id, no channel — and dispatches straight to git pull + process-kill + self-restart (deploy_update), git revert (deploy_rollback), and pkill/crontab mutation (deploy_cleanup). The wiring that existed (assistant_handler.py:236-246) invoked it for EVERY user message before classification with no operator check, so any workspace member DM'ing the bot could restart/rollback the trading host. Latent today only because the caller is dead (gap1-03); any revival as-is re-arms an unauthenticated code-deployment path. The repo's own fail-closed pattern (operator_tokens.is_operator_token_message: operator_user_id + channel allowlist, unconfigured=deny) proves the standard and its absence here.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T5 (OWASP API5 Broken Function Level Authorization) + T8 round-6 Agent Audit rule (a): boundary-aware analysis — elevated scrutiny at privileged sinks. Contrast pattern: operator_tokens.py:87-95 ('fail-closed: unconfigured operator accepts nothing').
confirmed.suggested_fix: Change signature to handle_deploy_command(text, user_id, channel); fail-closed against settings.slack_operator_user_id and the approvals channel, mirroring is_operator_token_message; must merge before or with any gap1-03 rewiring.
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified: handle_deploy_command(text) at self_update.py:436 dispatches on message text alone to deploy_update (git pull + SIGTERM restart via _schedule_restart, lines 128-212/262-357), deploy_rollback (git revert + restart, line 217), and deploy_cleanup — zero caller authorization in the function or its only (historical) caller assistant_handler.py:236-246, which ran it for every message with only a token-budget check. Caller is dead (assistant_handler imported nowhere; live path assistant_lifecycle→streaming_integration never calls it), so latent not live. Contrast pattern confirmed at operator_tokens.py:87-88 (fail-closed operator check). Not a duplicate: no register mention; prior phase-4.17.9 self_update audit covered only syntax/paths, not authz. P1 (latent-serious) is honest — any gap1-03 rewiring re-arms an unauthenticated code-deploy path.

confirmed.title: HITL governance layer is fiction: governance.py has zero live importers, its sole consumer imports five nonexistent names, and the live path has no rate limiting or audit logging
confirmed.claim: governance.py (AuditLogger, RateLimiter, HumanInTheLoopManager, ContentDisclaimer) is imported only by dead assistant_handler.py — and that import names AuditRecord, get_audit_logger, get_token_tracker, classify_error, get_fallback_message, NONE of which exist in governance.py (defined names: AuditLog, AuditLogger, HumanInTheLoopManager, ContentDisclaimer, RateLimiter, log_agent_response), so even the dead path would ImportError at line 200. Consequence on the LIVE path: streaming_integration has no rate/budget/limit logic at all (grep-verified), so every Slack assistant message hits metered Opus-4.8 MAS calls unthrottled and unaudited, while the module docstring and its slack.dev agent-governance reference advertise both controls. Even if wired, audit_logs is an unbounded in-memory list that is never persisted.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T6 (Anthropic building-effective-agents: 'add complexity only when it demonstrably improves outcomes' — dead scaffolding masquerading as controls) and T5 API4 Unrestricted Resource Consumption (unthrottled inbound path to a metered LLM). Module's own reference https://docs.slack.dev/ai/agent-governance. Not covered by pysvc-01/02 (streaming_integration findings) — this anchors the governance module contradiction.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Delete governance.py + dead assistant_handler consumers, then implement the two real controls where the live path runs (streaming_integration): a minimal per-user request/token budget and a persisted (JSONL or BQ) audit record per interaction; or explicitly document that no governance layer exists.
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified in full: governance.py defines only AuditLog/AuditLogger/HumanInTheLoopManager/ContentDisclaimer/RateLimiter/log_agent_response, while its sole consumer assistant_handler.py imports five nonexistent names (AuditRecord, get_audit_logger, get_token_tracker, classify_error, get_fallback_message) at lines 200-203, 361, 743, 751 — guaranteed ImportError if ever executed. assistant_handler itself is dead: app.py registers only assistant_lifecycle (app.py:18,33), which routes to streaming_integration; no importer of assistant_handler exists (self_update.py:178 is a path string only). Grep confirms streaming_integration.py has zero rate/budget/limit/throttle logic (sole hit line 160 is unrelated), so the live Slack path to metered MAS calls is unthrottled and unaudited despite the module docstring advertising both controls. audit_logs at line 64 is an unbounded, never-persisted in-memory list. No duplicate in confirmed_findings.json, audit_phase69, or harness_proposals.json. P2 honest: internal whitelisted Socket Mode surface, so debt not live security.

confirmed.title: Sync HTTP + subprocess calls block the bot's event loop up to ~41s inside async handlers (App Home render, reaction push, status)
confirmed.claim: async update_app_home calls sync _get_live_data(), which does two blocking httpx.get (3s each) plus three subprocess.run for openclaw CLI (timeouts 10s/15s/10s) — worst case ~41s of event-loop block every time the Home tab opens. commands.py compounds it: handle_reaction blocks up to 30s on sync git push, handle_any_message calls sync _read_status (urllib 5s + subprocess 5s). While blocked, the single-loop Socket-Mode bot cannot ack actions/commands (Slack 3s ack deadline), deliver operator tokens, run the watchdog, or answer WebSocket pings — the whole operator control plane stalls; the correct pattern already exists in the same package (scheduler.py:487 `await _asyncio.to_thread(_git_today)`).
confirmed.evidence:
app_home.py:350,356:
    async def update_app_home(client: WebClient, event, logger):
            data = _get_live_data()
app_home.py:57,78-80,84-86 (inside sync _get_live_data):
        r = httpx.get("http://localhost:8000/api/health", timeout=3)
        gw = subprocess.run(
            ["openclaw", "gateway", "status"],
            capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=10,
        cron_out = subprocess.run(
            ["openclaw", "cron", "list", "--json"],
            capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=15,
commands.py:342-344:
                result = subprocess.check_output(
                    ["git", "push", "origin", "main"],
                    cwd=str(_PROJECT_ROOT), text=True, timeout=30,
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T1 rule 1 (blocking I/O never inside async def — 'the No.1 FastAPI production defect class', same asyncio principle; round-3 query 'Python asyncio pitfalls blocking event loop run_in_executor') + S6 (Slack Bolt Python Socket Mode: await ack() discipline).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Wrap _get_live_data, _read_status, and the git push in await asyncio.to_thread(...) (or convert probes to httpx.AsyncClient), matching the existing scheduler.py:487 idiom.
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified line-by-line: app_home.py:349-356 registers async update_app_home on AsyncApp (app.py:29, AsyncSocketModeHandler) and calls sync _get_live_data(), which does two sync httpx.get(timeout=3) at :57/:64 plus three subprocess.run openclaw CLI calls at :78(10s)/:84(15s)/:106(10s) — worst-case ~41s of event-loop block, exactly as claimed. commands.py compounds it: async handle_reaction runs sync git-push subprocess.check_output(timeout=30) at :342-346, and async handle_any_message calls sync _read_status (:313; subprocess at :60 + urllib urlopen timeout=5 at :74-76). No to_thread/executor/lazy-listener wrapper neutralizes any of these; the correct idiom exists in the same package (scheduler.py:487 await _asyncio.to_thread(_git_today)), so this is an inconsistency, not a convention. Not a duplicate: the phase-69 register item (register.md:58, orchestrator.py:1810) is the same defect family but a different file and different process (backend orchestrator, not the Slack bot); no register entry covers app_home.py/commands.py. P2 is honest — real operator-control-plane stall risk (Slack 3s ack deadline, approvals/kill commands delayed) but timeout-bounded, event-triggered, and typically far below worst case.

confirmed.title: App Home model dropdowns let any workspace user silently repoint MAS agent models — no allowlist, no persistence, no audit trail
confirmed.claim: The four agent_model_change_* actions mutate AGENT_CONFIGS[agent].model for any user who opens App Home: mas_main/mas_qa could be dropped from claude-opus-4-8 to claude-haiku-4-5 (degrading the quality-critical evaluators that model_tiers.py:71-74 says are 'DELIBERATELY KEPT on Opus 4.8'), or communication bumped to Opus at ~10x metered cost. There is no operator check, no confirmation, the only audit is a log line, and the change is process-local and ephemeral (lost on bot restart, never propagated to the backend process or model_tiers) — so the advertised governance 'control' is both ungated and misleading about its own scope.
confirmed.evidence:
app_home.py:378,380,383-384,389-392:
    async def _handle_model_change(ack, body, client, agent_type_str):
        await ack()
        selected = body["actions"][0]["selected_option"]["value"]
        user_id = body["user"]["id"]
        if agent_type and agent_type in AGENT_CONFIGS:
            old_model = AGENT_CONFIGS[agent_type].model
            AGENT_CONFIGS[agent_type].model = selected
            logger.info(f"[RETRY] Agent model changed: {agent_type.value} {old_model} -> {selected} (by {user_id})")
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T5 (OWASP API5 function-level authorization) + backend/config/model_tiers.py:59-74 (operator-reasoned deliberate pins, phase-59.1) which this unaudited dropdown overrides. llmeng-06 covers model_tiers.py itself, not this mutation surface.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Gate the four actions on settings.slack_operator_user_id (fail-closed); write an audit JSONL line on change; label the control 'process-local, resets on restart' or persist via the model_tiers override path.
confirmed.verify_reason: Confirmed. app_home.py:378-395 mutates AGENT_CONFIGS[agent_type].model from any App Home dropdown interaction with zero user gating; the operator-gate pattern (operator_tokens.py, used in commands.py:108) is absent here. The mutation is live in-process (MAS orchestrator and streaming_integration read AGENT_CONFIGS at call time in the same bot process) yet ephemeral and never persisted to model_tiers.py, whose lines 59-74 document deliberate operator pins this dropdown silently overrides. No duplicate in phase-69/73 registers or harness_proposals.json; llmeng-06 covers model_tiers.py itself. P2 honest: private single-operator Socket Mode workspace and restart-reversibility keep it below P1, but it is a real authz/governance gap worth fixing.

confirmed.title: deploy_update never restores the safety stash on success — uncommitted tracked changes (incl. git-tracked operator_tokens.jsonl appends) silently vanish
confirmed.claim: Step 2 stashes local changes; `git stash pop` runs only on pull-failure (:156) and already-up-to-date (:167). On the success path (pull succeeded, new commit) the stash is abandoned, so every successful deploy strips uncommitted tracked modifications from the working tree. handoff/operator_tokens.jsonl IS git-tracked (verified `git ls-files --error-unmatch` returns it), so operator reply-tokens appended since the last commit — the ONLY away-ops token-ingestion record — would be swallowed and the away session would never act on them; same for other tracked handoff state and operator hot-fixes. The docstring's safety claim ('Backs up current files before pulling') is inverted: the backup is created and then orphaned. Latent only because the plane is unreachable (gap1-03).
confirmed.evidence:
self_update.py:147-156,165-167:
    # Step 2: Stash local changes
    _, stash_out = _run(["git", "stash"])
    if "No local changes" not in stash_out:
        lines.append(f"• Stashed local changes")
    # Step 3: Pull
    code, pull_output = _run(["git", "pull", "origin", "main"], timeout=60)
    if code != 0:
        # Restore stash on failure
        _run(["git", "stash", "pop"])
    if old_commit.strip() == new_commit.strip():
        ...
        _run(["git", "stash", "pop"])
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T8 rule 2/4 (verbatim evidence + cheap empirical gate: git ls-files proof) and operator_tokens.py:1-25 FO-2 contract (tokens JSONL is the sole ingestion authority for away sessions — losing its uncommitted tail defeats the chain). Distinct from pending 72.1.1 (sentinel token reconciliation reads the file; this destroys its tail).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Pop the stash on the success path before scheduling restart (report conflicts instead of dropping), or refuse to deploy while tracked handoff/*.jsonl files are dirty; at minimum report the stash ref in the Slack response.
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified verbatim: git stash at self_update.py:148; stash pop only at :156 (pull failure) and :167 (already up to date). The success path (new commit, lines 170-214) and the syntax-error abort path (:194-197) never pop, so every successful deploy silently strips uncommitted tracked modifications from the working tree (recoverable only by manually inspecting git stash list). handoff/operator_tokens.jsonl is git-tracked (git ls-files confirms) and operator_tokens.py's docstring states it is the ONLY token-ingestion mechanism for headless away sessions, so a stashed tail of appended reply-tokens would never be acted on. deploy_update is reachable via assistant_handler.py:238-239 (latent only per gap1-03). Aggravating: the project's own standing rule at handoff/current/research_brief_62.2.md:82 says 'never git stash (hook-append conflict, standing rule)' — this code violates it. No duplicate in confirmed_findings.json, audit_phase69, or other registers; distinct from pending 72.1.1 (which reads the file, this destroys its uncommitted tail). P2 honest: latent (plane unreachable), data recoverable from orphaned stash, but silent loss of the sole away-ops ingestion record plus a documented-rule violation.

confirmed.title: Deploy pipeline mechanics unsound: dry-run fetch staleness, hardcoded origin/main pull, 7-file syntax allowlist (includes dead files, omits live ones), bare `python`, quote-corrupting .env parser in restart script
confirmed.claim: Five mechanical defects in the (currently unreachable) deploy plane: (a) deploy_status computes behind-count after `git fetch --dry-run`, which by definition changes nothing, so 'Up to date with remote' reflects the LAST real fetch; (b) deploy_update pulls hardcoded `origin main` while status/diff use the current branch — on a non-main checkout it merges main into that branch; (c) the post-pull syntax gate checks 7 hardcoded files including two dead ones (assistant_handler.py, governance.py) while omitting live modules (scheduler.py, formatters.py, streaming_integration.py, app.py, self_update.py itself), so a syntax error there restarts into a crash-retry-give-up sequence; (d) syntax check and any PATH-dependent `python` invocation should use sys.executable; (e) the generated restart script's hand-rolled .env parser keeps surrounding quotes (`env[k.strip()] = v.strip()`), unlike python-dotenv, feeding corrupted secret values to the restarted bot if .env values are quoted.
confirmed.evidence:
self_update.py:95-96,153,189,314-316:
    _run(["git", "fetch", "--dry-run"])
    _, behind = _run(["git", "rev-list", "--count", f"{branch}..origin/{branch}"])
    code, pull_output = _run(["git", "pull", "origin", "main"], timeout=60)
            code, err = _run(["python", "-c", f"import py_compile; py_compile.compile('{fpath}', doraise=True)"])
                if '=' in line and not line.startswith('#'):
                    k, v = line.split('=', 1)
                    env[k.strip()] = v.strip()
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T8 evidence discipline + T2 (Python idioms: sys.executable over PATH `python`); git official semantics for fetch --dry-run ('show what would be done, without making any changes'); pydantic-settings/dotenv quote-stripping behavior the deployment relies on (settings.py env_file loading per confirmed_findings.json os.environ analysis).
confirmed.suggested_fix: If the plane is revived (gap1-03): real `git fetch origin` before rev-list; pull the CURRENT branch or refuse off-main; py_compile the changed files from `git diff --name-only old..HEAD` with sys.executable; reuse dotenv in the restart script. If removed, this dies with the module.
confirmed.verify_reason: All five mechanical defects verified in backend/slack_bot/self_update.py: (a) :95 fetch --dry-run before rev-list makes deploy_status's behind-count reflect the last real fetch (deploy_diff at :108 does a real fetch, confirming this is a bug); (b) :153 hardcoded `git pull origin main` vs branch-aware status/diff; (c) :176-184 allowlist includes dead assistant_handler.py and governance.py (assistant_handler imported nowhere live; governance imported only by dead assistant_handler — app.py's register_governance comes from app_home) while omitting live scheduler.py, formatters.py, streaming_integration.py, streaming_handler.py, and self_update.py itself — minor drift: the finding wrongly lists app.py as omitted, but it IS in the list at :177; (d) :189 bare `python` despite sys.executable being used at :360 in the same module; on macOS without bare `python`, _run returns code 1 and every deploy is spuriously blocked as a 'syntax error'; (e) :313-316 hand-rolled parser keeps surrounding quotes, and backend/.env has 1 quoted assignment (of 51), which would reach the restarted bot corrupted since os.environ outranks env_file in pydantic-settings. No guard neutralizes any of these; plane is unreachable (only caller is dead assistant_handler.py:238), which caps severity at P3. No prior-phase register duplicate; gap1-03 is a same-batch sibling covering unreachability, not these mechanics.

confirmed.title: Three orphan modules (mcp_tools mock executor, streaming_handler, context_management) — ~740 more lines of dead 'Slack AI Agent Phase 3/4/5' scaffolding, with mock tool responses
confirmed.claim: mcp_tools.py (247 ln), streaming_handler.py (243 ln), context_management.py (249 ln) have zero importers across backend/, scripts/, and tests (grep-verified). mcp_tools' MCPToolExecutor is worse than dead: its tools return hardcoded mock strings ('Found messages matching: ...'), so if ever wired it would feed fabricated tool results to the LLM. Together with dead governance.py and assistant_handler.py (~1,100 ln, gap1-03/05) that is ~1,800 lines of inert control-plane scaffolding that maintenance keeps servicing (phase-56.2 F-14 edited dead governance.py; self_update's syntax gate validates two dead files).
confirmed.evidence:
mcp_tools.py:134,138-139,161:
    async def execute_tool(self, tool_name: str, tool_input: Dict[str, Any]) -> str:
        In production, would make actual API call to Slack MCP server.
        For now, returns mock response.
        return f"Found messages matching: {query}"
Import-graph greps (backend/, scripts/, tests/): 'mcp_tools' -> only self-references; 'context_management|streaming_handler' -> zero hits outside the modules themselves.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T6 (Anthropic simplicity principle: 'consider adding complexity only when it demonstrably improves outcomes') + T8 round-6 Agent Audit (mock/dead tool executors are agent-boundary confusion surface); consistent with phase-71 register precedent of pruning unused scaffolding.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Delete the three modules (fold into the gap1-03/05 revive-or-remove decision for assistant_handler/governance); add a cheap CI orphan-import check for backend/slack_bot/.
confirmed.verify_reason: All claims check out: the three modules total exactly 739 lines (247/243/249); repo-wide grep finds zero importers of mcp_tools, streaming_handler, or context_management outside mcp_tools.py itself (not even dead assistant_handler.py imports them); MCPToolExecutor's four handlers all return hardcoded mock strings ('Found messages matching: ...', etc.) with '# TODO: Call Slack MCP server' comments, so wiring it would feed fabricated tool results to the LLM; phase-60.1 archive confirms maintenance repinned mcp_tools.py:204 despite the module being dead; self_update.py:178,180 does validate dead assistant_handler.py/governance.py. Not covered by any phase-69/70/71/72/73 register item (only archive/log mentions), not intentional per .claude/rules/backend-slack-bot.md (its Key Modules list omits all three). P3 honest: dead-code debt with no live-path effect.

confirmed.title: append_operator_token re-validates parseability but not identity, despite claiming to be the 'last line of defense' for the sole away-ops token authority
confirmed.claim: The writer for handoff/operator_tokens.jsonl — per FO-2 the ONLY authority that gates backend/.env writes by away sessions — accepts user/channel verbatim and never re-checks them against slack_operator_user_id / allowed channels; only parseability is double-checked. Authorization exists solely in the Bolt matcher (commands.py:106-109). Any future or alternate caller (a new handler, a script, a test helper left wired) can append records that away sessions will treat as operator decisions. The docstring's own 'last line of defense' framing sets the standard the code does not meet.
confirmed.evidence:
operator_tokens.py:98-107:
async def append_operator_token(
    *, text: str, user: str, channel: str, ts: str, event_id: str | None = None
) -> tuple[int, dict] | None:
    """Dedupe-check -> append -> return (1-based line number, record).

    Returns None for duplicates or unparseable text (the matcher should have
    filtered the latter; double-checked here because append is the last line
    of defense for file integrity).
    """
    parsed = parse_operator_token(text)
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T8 round-6 Agent Audit rule (a) (boundary-aware analysis: privilege checks at the sink, not only the ingress) + the module's own FO-2 contract (operator_tokens.py:21-25). Distinct from pending 72.1.1 (sentinel reads reconciliation; this is write-side identity).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Pass operator_user_id and allowed_channels into append_operator_token and fail-closed (return None + warning log) when the record's user/channel do not match — the same check the matcher performs, repeated at the sink.
confirmed.verify_reason: Confirmed. append_operator_token (operator_tokens.py:98-132) re-checks only parseability and dedupe; user/channel are written verbatim with no check against slack_operator_user_id or allowed channels. Authorization exists solely in the Bolt matcher chain (is_operator_token_message, wired at commands.py:106-109; sole caller commands.py:119), and downstream consumers (unapplied_tokens, away-ops prompts/runbook) validate token keys via KNOWN_TOKEN_ENV_MAP but never identity — so any future direct caller of the sink appends records away sessions will honor. Not a duplicate: pending 72.1.1 is the sentinel's read-side approved-but-unapplied reconciliation, a distinct consume-side gap; no register item covers write-side identity. Two caveats keep severity at P3: the docstring's 'last line of defense' is explicitly scoped to file integrity (parseability/dedupe), which the code does meet, and a sink-side check only guards accidental mis-wiring since any in-process caller could append to the unsigned JSONL directly. Defense-in-depth hardening consistent with the module's own fail-closed style (line 88); small fix, worth doing.

confirmed.title: Middleware auth gate keyed only on Google creds — passkey-only (or any non-Google) config disables all route protection (fail-open)
confirmed.claim: The middleware decides whether to enforce auth from `hasAuthProvider = !!(AUTH_GOOGLE_ID && AUTH_GOOGLE_SECRET)` alone. When Google is not configured it returns early (no redirect), serving every protected route unauthenticated. But Passkey/WebAuthn is a fully independent provider wired only in auth.ts and offered on the login page, so a passkey-only deployment has `hasAuthProvider=false` and NO middleware auth at all. The early-return is also gratuitous: NextAuth JWT session validation (`req.auth` from the encrypted cookie) needs no provider configured, so the gate would work even with an empty providers list.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md §T5 (OWASP API2 Broken Authentication — 'audit every router actually mounts' the auth gate; https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0x11-t10/); .claude/rules/frontend.md Auth Flow ('src/middleware.ts protects all routes except /login and /api/auth/*'). A gate that opens when a specific provider env is unset violates fail-closed auth design.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Gate on whether ANY provider is configured (include Passkey) or, better, drop the provider check entirely and enforce `req.auth` unconditionally except for the explicit public paths; keep only the `LIGHTHOUSE_SKIP_AUTH` bypass. If a true dev-open mode is desired, drive it from an explicit `DEV_DISABLE_AUTH`-style flag rather than inferring it from Google-cred absence.
confirmed.verify_reason: Evidence verbatim at middleware.ts:7,24-26. Passkey is unconditionally wired (auth.ts:13 appends Passkey with no env gate) and offered on login/page.tsx, yet the middleware's only enforcement gate keys on Google creds — passkey-only or env-drifted deployments serve all routes unauthenticated (fail-open). Sub-claim also verified: req.auth is JWT-cookie decoding (session.strategy jwt in auth.config.ts) needing no provider, so the gate is gratuitous. Middleware is the sole route protection per .claude/rules/frontend.md; backend Bearer is just the forwarded session cookie (api.ts:52-58), nothing re-imposes the gate. Only LIGHTHOUSE_SKIP_AUTH is a documented intentional bypass; the !hasAuthProvider leg contradicts frontend.md's stated auth flow. No duplicate in phase 69-73 registers (grep of handoff/ for hasAuthProvider/middleware/AUTH_GOOGLE found none). Not P0 because live deployment has Google creds configured (secrets_rotation_check.json), so enforcement is active today; latent-serious fail-open auth = P1.

confirmed.title: Empty/unset ALLOWED_EMAILS silently disables the sign-in allowlist — any Google account mints a 30-day session
confirmed.claim: `allowedEmails` defaults to `process.env.ALLOWED_EMAILS || ""`, which parses to an empty array when the env var is unset/empty. The signIn callback only enforces the whitelist `if (allowedEmails.length > 0 ...)`, so with no ALLOWED_EMAILS the check is skipped and ANY Google account that reaches the login page is admitted and issued a 30-day JWT session — despite the login footer stating 'Access restricted to authorized users'. There is no fail-closed default. The backend shares the identical fail-open default (settings.py:570), so the two allowlists collapse together rather than backstopping each other.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md §T5 (OWASP API2 Broken Authentication + secrets/allowlist rules); .claude/rules/security.md 'Email whitelist enforced on both frontend (NextAuth callback) and backend (ALLOWED_EMAILS)'. Fail-open allowlist contradicts the documented convention.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Fail closed: if no ALLOWED_EMAILS is configured, reject all sign-ins (return false) unless an explicit `AUTH_ALLOW_ALL=1` dev opt-in is set, mirroring the backend's explicit DEV_DISABLE_AUTH pattern. At minimum log a loud startup warning when the allowlist is empty.
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified: auth.config.ts:10-13 parses ALLOWED_EMAILS to an empty array when unset and lines 46-50 skip the whitelist when empty, so any verified Google account gets a 30-day JWT; middleware authorized() only checks session existence, no backstop. Backend is identically fail-open (settings.py:570 'Empty = allow all authenticated users.', auth.py:209-214), so both allowlists collapse together, contradicting .claude/rules/security.md:10 and ARCHITECTURE.md:1023 ('whitelist enforced on both frontend and backend'). Not fixed, not in any phase-69+ register. Downgraded P1->P2: the backend field description documents empty=allow-all as intended semantics; exploitation requires ALLOWED_EMAILS to be dropped from the operator's env (docs list it as standard config in backend/.env and frontend/.env.local, which exists) AND network reach to a localhost-only, single-Mac, paper-only deployment, plus sign-in through the operator's own Google OAuth client. Real fail-open hardening gap worth the suggested fail-closed default, but latent misconfiguration debt, not latent-serious.

confirmed.title: Backend get_current_user allowlist is fail-open by default (unaudited auth.py) — 'Empty = allow all authenticated users'
confirmed.claim: The backend's real data-plane gate skips the email whitelist entirely when `settings.allowed_emails` is falsy, and that field defaults to empty string (settings.py:570: 'Empty = allow all authenticated users'). This file was not covered by the security auditors (security-01/03/04 are main.py), yet it is the enforcement point behind the JWE decrypt. It is defense-in-depth (a token must still decrypt with AUTH_SECRET), but combined with the frontend fail-open (gap2-02) the composite is a full bypass: an unset allowlist on both layers admits any Google user to live paper-trading/kill-switch data.
confirmed.evidence:
207	    # Check email whitelist
208	    email = payload.get("email", "")
209	    if settings.allowed_emails:
210	        allowed = [e.strip().lower() for e in settings.allowed_emails.split(",") if e.strip()]
211	        if allowed and email.lower() not in allowed:
212	            logger.warning(f"auth_denied: email={email}")
213	            raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Authentication required")
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md §T5 (API2; brief notes get_current_user is 'wired in main.py' but the allowlist branch itself was never opened); .claude/rules/security.md 'Email whitelist enforced on ... backend (ALLOWED_EMAILS)'. Documented-fail-open default violates the whitelist convention.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make the empty-allowlist case explicit and loud: require an opt-in env flag to run with no whitelist, and emit a startup WARNING. Align the semantics with the frontend fix so both layers fail closed together.
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified: auth.py:209 skips the whitelist entirely when settings.allowed_emails is falsy, and settings.py:570 defaults it to "" ("Empty = allow all authenticated users"). get_current_user is the global enforcement point via main.py:427-437 middleware, so an unset allowlist admits any Google-authenticated JWE holder to all data-plane routes. Not neutralized by any guard; the same file's AUTH_SECRET-missing fail-open was hardened to hard-fail + explicit DEV_DISABLE_AUTH=1 opt-in (auth.py:155-167), establishing project precedent that this class of documented fail-open is a defect, and .claude/rules/security.md requires backend whitelist enforcement. No duplicate in phase-69/70/71/72/73 registers (grepped confirmed_findings.json, design_audit_burndown_69.md, harness_proposals.json, audit_phase69). P2 is honest: misconfiguration-dependent, defense-in-depth remains (valid AUTH_SECRET decrypt still required), single-operator deployment normally sets ALLOWED_EMAILS per AGENTS.md.

confirmed.title: Google email_verified rejection is dead code — reads `account` (aliased 'profile'), not the OIDC profile, so it never fires
confirmed.claim: The signIn callback destructures only `{ user, account }` and then aliases `const profile = account`. `email_verified` is a claim on the OIDC profile / ID-token, NOT on the `account` object (which holds tokens/provider/scope). So `profile.email_verified` is always `undefined`, making the guard `!undefined && undefined !== undefined` evaluate to `false` — the unverified-email rejection can never execute. The control that looks like it blocks unverified Google emails is silently inert.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md §T5 (API2 Broken Authentication — verify identity claims); Auth.js signIn-callback contract exposes `profile` separately from `account` (https://authjs.dev/reference/core/types#profile). A security check reading the wrong object is a latent auth defect.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Destructure `profile` in the callback signature (`async signIn({ user, account, profile })`) and read `profile?.email_verified` from the actual OIDC profile; remove the misleading `account as ... profile` alias.
confirmed.verify_reason: Confirmed dead code. signIn callback destructures only {user, account} and aliases account as 'profile'; email_verified is an OIDC profile/ID-token claim, never on the Auth.js Account object (token-endpoint fields only), so profile.email_verified is always undefined and the guard `!undefined && undefined !== undefined` evaluates true && false = false — line 43's return false is unreachable. auth.ts spreads authConfig without overriding callbacks.signIn, and repo-wide grep shows this is the only email_verified reference, so no wrapper neutralizes or duplicates the check. The `as Record<string, unknown>` cast confirms the typed Account lacks the field. Partially mitigated by the ALLOWED_EMAILS allowlist (lines 46-50), which keeps this at P2 latent-debt rather than P1: exploitation requires an unverified Google identity claiming an allowlisted email. No register duplicate in confirmed_findings.json or phase 69-73 artifacts; no CLAUDE.md/.claude/rules exemption. Fix as suggested: add `profile` to the callback signature and read profile?.email_verified.

confirmed.title: JWT session strategy + 30-day maxAge with no server-side revocation on a trade/kill-switch cockpit
confirmed.claim: `session.strategy: "jwt"` with `maxAge: 30 days` means sessions are self-contained encrypted tokens; NextAuth never consults the Prisma Session table (schema.prisma:43-51 is dead under jwt strategy). A copied/leaked session cookie therefore remains valid for up to 30 days with no revocation path — sign-out only clears the local cookie. This authorizes an app that can flip the kill-switch, flatten positions, and change risk limits. No `updateAge` is set either, so an active user's window slides forward indefinitely.
confirmed.evidence:
30	  session: {
31	    strategy: "jwt",
32	    maxAge: 30 * 24 * 60 * 60, // 30 days
33	  },
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md §T5 (API2 session management) + recency #10 (next-auth v5-beta on a maintenance-mode library); OWASP API2 guidance on session lifetime/revocation. The unused Prisma Session table is documented dead weight under jwt strategy (Auth.js session docs).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Shorten maxAge for this sensitive surface (e.g. 1–7 days) and set an explicit `updateAge`; for true revocability switch to database sessions (the Session table + adapter already exist) or add a token-version/`jti` denylist checked in the jwt/session callbacks.
confirmed.verify_reason: Evidence verbatim at auth.config.ts:30-33. Explicit strategy:"jwt" overrides the PrismaAdapter default in auth.ts, so the prisma Session table (schema.prisma:43-51) is dead weight and no server-side session record exists. No jti/token-version/denylist check in the jwt/session callbacks, and the backend (backend/api/auth.py::get_current_user) independently decrypts the same JWE cookie checking only email whitelist + exp — so a leaked cookie grants cockpit UI AND direct backend API access (kill-switch/flatten/risk-limits) for up to 30 days with no revocation path; sign-out only clears the local cookie. Not a duplicate: no session-lifetime/revocation item in phase-69 register, confirmed.json, or confirmed_findings.json; security.md documents the JWE scheme but does not sanction the 30-day lifetime. Minor nit: Auth.js ignores updateAge under jwt strategy (sliding expiry is inherent; AuthProvider refetches every 15 min), so that part of the suggested fix is cosmetic — shorter maxAge, database sessions, or a token-version denylist are the real remedies. P2 is honest: exploitation requires prior cookie theft (HttpOnly, __Secure- in prod, CORS limited to localhost/Tailscale), making this hardening debt on a sensitive surface rather than a live P0/P1 hole.

confirmed.title: Allowlist is skipped for principals with no email — `&& user.email` short-circuits (passkey users, User.email nullable)
confirmed.claim: Even when ALLOWED_EMAILS is configured, the guard `if (allowedEmails.length > 0 && user.email)` short-circuits whenever `user.email` is null/undefined, falling through to `return true` (admit). `User.email` is nullable in the schema (`email String? @unique`), and passkey/WebAuthn principals can lack an email claim, so a passkey user with no email bypasses the allowlist entirely rather than being rejected. An emailless principal is precisely the case a whitelist should reject, not wave through.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md §T5 (API2 Broken Authentication); schema.prisma:13 `email String? @unique` (nullable). Fail-open on the missing-attribute path contradicts .claude/rules/security.md whitelist enforcement.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Treat missing email as a denial when an allowlist is active: `if (allowedEmails.length > 0) { if (!user.email || !allowedEmails.includes(user.email.toLowerCase())) return false; }`.
confirmed.verify_reason: Code defect is real: `allowedEmails.length > 0 && user.email` at auth.config.ts:46 falls through to `return true` for an emailless principal, and this signIn callback is the only frontend allowlist gate (auth.ts:11-15 spreads it for the Passkey provider; the `authorized` middleware callback only checks session existence). However the claimed exploit path is unreachable in the shipped flows: the installed @auth/core WebAuthn provider's default getUserInfo (webauthn.js:82-97) requires an email to register (inferWebAuthnOptions only returns "register" when user info exists), and Google always supplies email — so no emailless passkey principal can be created. Additionally the backend allowlist is fail-closed (backend/api/auth.py:207-212 denies empty email), backstopping all data access per security.md:10's dual-layer enforcement. Real defense-in-depth mismatch worth the one-line fail-closed fix, but with no live failure scenario and a fail-closed backend backstop it is hardening polish: downgrade P2 -> P3. No duplicate in phase-69/70/71/72/73 registers.

confirmed.title: Weekly meta-evolution cron is never registered on any live scheduler — the 'LIVE weekly path' never fires
confirmed.claim: register_meta_evolution_cron has zero production call sites (only tests/verify_phase_25_P.py and tests/scheduler/test_meta_cron.py reference it; `git log --all -S register_meta_evolution_cron -- backend/main.py` is empty), so run_meta_evolution_cycle — the cron-budget allocation, provider rebalance, and the phase-25.P weekly Slack summary that 'closes audit bucket 24.5 F-5(g)' — has never executed on a schedule, while formatters.py:918 ('Invoked by backend/meta_evolution/cron.py on Sunday'), harness_self_audit_report.py:89 ('offset from the meta_evolution 02:00 slot'), and harness_proposals.json:572 ('the live weekly meta-evolution cron') all assert it is live.
confirmed.evidence:
backend/main.py:268-280 (lifespan registers ONLY these two):
            init_scheduler(scheduler)
            scheduler.start()
            ...
                    register_harness_self_audit_cron(scheduler)
backend/api/paper_trading.py:1289-1296 init_scheduler adds only the daily paper job.
backend/slack_bot/formatters.py:918: "duration, errors. Invoked by `backend/meta_evolution/cron.py` on Sunday"
Grep (2026-07-19): register_meta_evolution_cron appears only in tests/verify_phase_25_P.py, tests/scheduler/test_meta_cron.py, backend/meta_evolution/cron.py, formatters.py docstring; `git log --all --oneline -S "register_meta_evolution_cron" -- backend/main.py` returns nothing.
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T8 methodology rule 4 (empirical grep gate) + role A7 'APScheduler duplicate-fire/misfire + shutdown' checkables (S5 https://apscheduler.readthedocs.io/en/3.x/faq.html); doc-vs-code drift class (brief A9). The phase-25.P audit-bucket closure and three doc artifacts assert a control that empirically does not exist.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Register it in main.py lifespan next to register_harness_self_audit_cron (same fail-open pattern, behind a default-ON settings flag), or explicitly retire it: delete the Sunday-invocation claims in formatters.py/harness_self_audit_report.py and reopen audit bucket 24.5 F-5(g). Pair with gap3-11's misfire_grace_time/coalesce fix when wiring.
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified: register_meta_evolution_cron (cron.py:43) has zero production call sites — main.py lifespan (263-289) registers only the paper-trading job, cron-registry hook, and register_harness_self_audit_cron; no other module or script registers the weekly job, so run_meta_evolution_cycle and its phase-25.P Slack summary (cron.py:153-177, 'Closes audit bucket 24.5 F-5(g)') have never fired on schedule. Doc drift confirmed at formatters.py:916-918, harness_self_audit_report.py:89, harness_proposals.json:572, and the 25.P live_check ('After next Sunday cron...') which could never have occurred yet the step is marked done. Not a duplicate: audit_phase69/features_vetted.json:373 covers the adjacent orphaned directive_rewriter path and itself assumes the cron is live. However P1 overstates it: masterplan notes for 10.7.6 ('Live wiring into start_scheduler() is a follow-up one-liner'), 10.7.8 ('honest about current wiring state: cron not yet wired'), and phase-45.0 DEFER-POST-PROD show the unwired state is a known, deliberate deferral; and the cycle is purely observational — cron_allocator.allocate/provider_rebalancer.allocate are pure compute with no writes and nothing consumes the result, so the only lost functionality is a weekly Slack summary/telemetry. Real defect (doc-vs-code drift + nominally-closed audit bucket 24.5 F-5(g)) worth fixing per the suggested one-liner or explicit retirement, but it is debt, not latent-serious: P2.

confirmed.title: Immutable governance risk limits have zero enforcement consumers; live kill-switch runs 2x looser on a runtime-mutable setting
confirmed.claim: None of the six RiskLimits fields is read by any enforcement code (grep for max_daily_loss_pct/max_position_notional_pct/etc. across backend/ outside governance yields zero hits); the live daily-loss kill-switch uses settings.paper_daily_loss_limit_pct=4.0 (double the governed 2% cap) which the operator can hot-mutate to anywhere in [0.5, 25.0] via PUT /api/settings — the exact runtime mutation the SIGHUP-ignore + os._exit(2) file-watcher exists to prevent — while the watcher kills the process only if the unread YAML changes; the lint step that was supposed to force migration is still WARN-only.
confirmed.evidence:
backend/governance/limits.yaml:30: max_daily_loss_pct: 0.02          # 2% daily loss kill-switch
backend/config/settings.py:528: paper_daily_loss_limit_pct: float = Field(4.0, description="Halt trading if intraday loss exceeds this %% of start-of-day NAV")

confirmed.title: weekly_ledger.append_row rewrites the whole TSV from a fail-open read — one transient read error or unescaped cell silently wipes all ledger history
confirmed.claim: append_row loads all rows via read_rows (which returns [] on ANY exception) and then non-atomically rewrites the entire file from that list, so a transient IO error, or any cell containing a tab/newline (notes are written unescaped, e.g. rollback._append_ledger_notes concatenates markers; _parse_row silently drops rows with wrong column counts), erases every prior week on the next write — and the ledger IS the Thursday/Friday slot-idempotency counter, so wiped history re-arms already-fired weekly slots.
confirmed.evidence:
backend/autoresearch/weekly_ledger.py:66-68:
    except Exception as exc:
        logger.warning("weekly_ledger: read fail-open: %r", exc)
        return []
weekly_ledger.py:89:        rows = read_rows(path=p)
weekly_ledger.py:108-110:
        body = "\n".join("\t".join(r[c] for c in COLUMNS) for r in rows) + ("\n" if rows else "")
        p.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        p.write_text(header + body, encoding="utf-8")
weekly_ledger.py:43-44 (_parse_row): if len(parts) != len(COLUMNS): return None
thursday_batch.py:7-8: "The ledger's idempotent per-week upsert IS the slot counter"
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T7 (golden/characterization durability discipline; 'money never gets lost' invariant class) and T8 empirical-evidence rule; atomic-write best practice (tmp + os.replace) per Python os docs https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.html#os.replace.
confirmed.suggested_fix: In append_row: distinguish 'file absent' from 'read failed' and fail CLOSED (return False, do not rewrite) on read errors; sanitize/escape tab+newline in every cell; write to a temp file in the same dir and os.replace() for atomicity.
confirmed.verify_reason: All cited code verified verbatim: read_rows fails open to [] on any exception (weekly_ledger.py:66-68), append_row rebuilds the full TSV from that list and non-atomically write_text's it (89, 107-110), cells are tab-joined unescaped, and _parse_row drops malformed rows (43-44). No caller neutralizes it: thursday_batch.py:75 and friday_promotion.py:124 call append_row directly and thursday_batch.py:7-8 confirms the ledger upsert IS the slot counter, so a transient read error at write time silently erases all prior weeks and re-arms fired slots. rollback._append_ledger_notes (rollback.py:144-165) concatenates notes unescaped, though current markers are tab-free so that vector is latent. The docstring's fail-open convention (phase-8.5.4/8.5.9) covers not crashing, not destroying history. Not a duplicate: the only prior ledger register item (audit_phase69/refuted.json, the "[]" reject-all slot guard) is a different, refuted defect. P2 honest: low-probability trigger on a small local file, but real durability debt with duplicate-spend blast radius; suggested fix (fail closed on read error, escape cells, tmp+os.replace) is correct.

confirmed.title: Promoter.write_to_registry supersedes the prior active strategy BEFORE writing the new row — a failed insert leaves zero active rows despite the 'atomically' docstring
confirmed.claim: The phase-25.R auto-switch path flips the prior active row to 'superseded' first, then attempts save_promoted_strategy with an independent fail-open except — so a BQ failure/crash between the two steps leaves the promoted_strategies registry with NO active/pending row and autonomous_loop.load_promoted_params silently reverts the live loop to optimizer_best.json params (a silent strategy change), contradicting the docstring's claim that it 'supersedes the prior active row atomically'; insert-first-then-supersede would shrink the failure window to a benign two-active state given the reader's ORDER BY promoted_at DESC. (Also currently has zero production callers — the 25.R 'red-line goal-c' closure is unwired.)
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T8 methodology + the phase-69 register's proven class 'non-atomic DELETE-then-INSERT' (bigquery_client.py:557 row) applied to a distinct un-audited code path; docstring-vs-code contract violation (brief A9 drift).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Reverse the order: write the new active row first, then supersede the prior on success only; or use a single MERGE. Fix the docstring either way. Note the unwired status in the register so the 25.R closure is re-opened or the caller is built.
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified at source: promoter.py supersedes the prior active row (lines 111-113) before writing the new active row (line 142), each in an independent fail-open try/except, so a save_promoted_strategy failure or crash between the two leaves zero active rows — contradicting the module docstring's 'supersedes the prior active row atomically' (lines 9-10) and the method docstring's 'atomically' (line 71). The reader path confirms impact: get_latest_promoted_strategy (bigquery_client.py:782-793) excludes 'superseded' by default, and load_promoted_params (autonomous_loop.py:46-74) silently falls back to optimizer_best.json. Insert-first would be benign given ORDER BY promoted_at DESC. No duplicate in phase-69 registers (the record_approval items in audit_phase69/refuted.json are a distinct path).

confirmed.title: strategy_selector anti-churn hysteresis inverts to always-switch when incumbent DSR is unknown, and writes delta_dsr=Infinity (invalid JSON) into the rotation audit log
confirmed.claim: When the incumbent dict is present but its dsr is None — exactly what rotation_runner._incumbent_dsr_from_optimizer_best produces on any read failure or missing 'dsr' key — inc_dsr becomes -inf, so delta = best_dsr - (-inf) = +inf always clears min_improvement and the selector returns switched=True with reason 'dsr_improvement' (a measured-improvement claim that was never measured), defeating the anti-churn hysteresis the module exists for; _persist_verdict then json.dumps the row, emitting the non-RFC-8259 token Infinity into rotation_log.jsonl, breaking strict JSON parsers/BQ ingestion of the verdict trail.
confirmed.evidence:
backend/autoresearch/strategy_selector.py:92:    inc_dsr = _safe_float(incumbent.get("dsr"), float("-inf")) if incumbent else float("-inf")
strategy_selector.py:126-128:

    if delta >= min_improvement:
        return _result(best, True, "dsr_improvement", round(delta, 6))
backend/autoresearch/rotation_runner.py:165-168: dsr = data.get("dsr"); return float(dsr) if dsr is not None else None / except Exception: return None
rotation_runner.py:228-229:
        with open(p, "a", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            f.write(json.dumps(row) + "\n")
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T8 evidence discipline; the module's own docstring (strategy_selector.py:7-11, jump-model 2024 anti-churn rationale) is violated on the None path; Python json docs note Infinity/NaN output 'is not valid JSON' (https://docs.python.org/3/library/json.html — allow_nan uses JavaScript extensions 'in violation of the JSON specification').
confirmed.suggested_fix: Treat unknown incumbent DSR as not-comparable: return a distinct 'incumbent_dsr_unknown' retain verdict (fail-closed for churn) instead of -inf sentinel arithmetic; clamp/None-ify non-finite delta before persisting; distinct from the queued 73.x work and the contested adapter row (different mechanism).
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified in code: strategy_selector.py:92 maps incumbent dsr=None to -inf (exact quoted line), and rotation_runner._resolve_incumbent (lines 171-197) supplies exactly that shape — a truthy incumbent dict with dsr=None whenever _incumbent_dsr_from_optimizer_best (lines 161-168) hits a read failure or missing 'dsr' key. Since _resolve_incumbent's own docstring says the incumbent NAME never equals a seed ID (incumbent_is_top unreachable), the delta path is routine: delta = best_dsr-(-inf) = +inf always clears min_improvement, returning switched=True/'dsr_improvement' with delta_dsr=round(inf,6)=inf — inverting the anti-churn hysteresis the module docstring (lines 7-11) exists to enforce. Executed json.dumps({'d': round(float('inf'),6)}) → {"d": Infinity}: _persist_verdict (rotation_runner.py:228-229) does write the non-RFC-8259 Infinity token to rotation_log.jsonl and hands the same row to bq_fn. No guard/wrapper neutralizes it; tests/autoresearch/test_strategy_selector.py has no dsr=None incumbent case. Not a duplicate: audit_phase69/contested.json's rotation row is the dead-params/blend-label mechanism, and 70-73 registers/briefs (incl. 73.5 champion-vs-incumbent OOS plan) address different work. Severity P2 is honest, not P1: the trigger is latent (live optimizer_best.json currently parses with dsr=0.9526) and the bake-off verdict is recorded at allocation_pct=0 audit-only with the deployment bridge deferred/operator-stopped, so no live-capital path consumes the bogus switch — but when triggered it fabricates a measured-improvement verdict and corrupts the JSONL/BQ audit trail, which is real debt worth fixing.

confirmed.title: slot_accounting default BQ helpers run query().result() with no timeout and construct a fresh bigquery.Client per call

slot_accounting.py:133-134: (same per-call Client construction in _default_bq_query_count)
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T4 checkable rule 3: 'Every client.query(...) and .result(...) carries a timeout (repo convention 30s — bigquery_client.py:533,765,798 confirmed compliant; audit the OTHER ~10 modules using BQ directly)' + S9 hang-on-network-drop risk (https://github.com/googleapis/python-bigquery/issues/2310).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add timeout=30 to .result() (and a job-level timeout on query); hoist a module-level lazily-created client or accept an injected client like the bq_insert_fn seam already allows.
confirmed.verify_reason: Evidence verified verbatim: line 139 runs client.query(sql, job_config=cfg).result() with no timeout, and both _default_bq_insert (117-118) and _default_bq_query_count (133-134) construct a fresh bigquery.Client per call, violating the repo's .result(timeout=30) convention confirmed in backend/db/bigquery_client.py:533 ('30s BQ timeout per CLAUDE.md'). One caveat: the finding's impact is overstated — no production code calls log_slot_usage/verify_weekly_invariant today (the phase-10.8.1 log_fn wiring seam is absent from trigger_thursday_batch and the other 3 routines; tests/autoresearch/test_slot_usage_wiring.py passes log_fn= to a signature that no longer accepts it, and tests inject capture fns so the default BQ helpers never execute). The defect is therefore latent/dormant, which caps it at P3 — but it is real, un-queued (no slot_accounting entry in confirmed_findings.json or audit_phase69), and worth the one-line timeout=30 fix since this module is the canonical phase-10 BQ sink and re-wiring would resurrect the hang risk.

confirmed.title: rollback auto-demote default sink paths are CWD-relative — the safety audit trail and idempotency state land wherever the caller's cwd happens to be
confirmed.claim: auto_demote_on_dd_breach (the 'no human approval — safety bright line' path) defaults its JSONL audit and idempotency-state sinks to Path('handoff/...') relative to the process CWD, unlike sibling modules that anchor to repo root via Path(__file__).resolve().parents[2]; invoked from any process not cd'd to the repo (launchd default cwd is /, the backend service, an API worker) the demotion state is written elsewhere or fail-opens — the idempotency check then never sees prior demotions, re-firing 'auto_demoted' events repeatedly and fragmenting the audit trail.

confirmed.suggested_fix: Anchor both defaults to Path(__file__).resolve().parents[2] / 'handoff' / ... like the sibling modules; keep the injectable overrides for tests.
confirmed.verify_reason: Evidence verified: rollback.py:28-29 defaults both sinks to CWD-relative Path('handoff/...'), and the idempotency check (lines 67-74) plus fail-open _load_state means a wrong-cwd invocation silently re-fires auto_demoted and fragments the audit trail. However, context softens it: (a) the 'unlike sibling modules' claim is wrong for the nearest sibling — monthly_champion_challenger.py:36 uses the identical CWD-relative default for the SAME state file, imported by monthly_approval_api.py:29, so this is the subsystem's own convention; (b) auto_demote_on_dd_breach has NO production caller (tests and scripts/harness only — never wired to a scheduler/API), so the defect is latent, not live; (c) all deployed launchd plists (backend, autoresearch, ablation) set WorkingDirectory to the repo root, neutralizing the launchd-cwd-/ scenario today. Real, unqueued, cheap-to-fix fragility on a self-described safety bright line, so worth fixing at P3 — but the fix must anchor monthly_champion_challenger.py's identical default in the same change (shared constant), or the demotion writer and the approval API could read different copies of the same state file.

confirmed.title: provider_rebalancer.rebalance() violates its own min_floor_usd guarantee — under-spent providers are cut below their guaranteed daily minimum
confirmed.claim: rebalance() locks every under-spent provider at its actual usage (pass1[provider] = used) with no floor clamp, so a provider that spent, e.g., $0.10 against a $1.00 min_floor_usd is granted $0.10 next period — directly contradicting the module title ('API-Credit Reallocator with per-provider USD floors') and provider_budget.yaml's 'min_floor_usd: guaranteed daily minimum', starving exactly the failover headroom the floors exist to protect (a healthy-but-idle fallback provider like openai floor $0.10 or anthropic floor $1.00 gets zeroed after any quiet day).
confirmed.evidence:
backend/meta_evolution/provider_rebalancer.py:212-215:
        else:
            # Under-spent: lock at actual usage; the rest becomes surplus.
            pass1[a.provider] = round(used, USD_PRECISION)
            surplus += a.clamped_budget - used
provider_rebalancer.py:1: "phase-10.7.5 API-Credit Reallocator with per-provider USD floors."
.claude/provider_budget.yaml:9: "min_floor_usd:      guaranteed daily minimum; sum(floors) MUST <= total"
confirmed.basis: research_brief_75.0.md T8 (contract-vs-implementation evidence rule); max-min fairness with floors requires the floor as the lower bound of any allocation (module's own cited WFQ/max-min basis, provider_rebalancer.py:8-19). Currently latent: run_meta_evolution_cycle calls only allocate(), never rebalance() — note alongside gap3-01.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Clamp pass-1 under-spent grants to max(used, floor): pass1[a.provider] = round(max(used, a.floor), USD_PRECISION) and compute surplus as clamped_budget - that value; add a unit test asserting no output ever drops below an enabled provider's floor.
confirmed.verify_reason: Verified at provider_rebalancer.py:214: pass1 locks under-spent providers at round(used) with no floor clamp; a.floor is carried in the Allocation dataclass but never read in rebalance() (only ceiling is), and surplus = clamped_budget - used redistributes the floor-protected headroom to demanding providers. No caller or wrapper re-applies floors, and tests/meta_evolution/test_provider_rebalancer.py asserts floors only for allocate(), never rebalance() outputs. Nuance: the behavior was contracted (phase-10.7.5 contract.md:99 'Pass 1: each provider gets min(used, ceiling) — locks in actual demand') and is docstring-documented, so it is a design decision — but that design self-contradicts the module's own title ('with per-provider USD floors') and provider_budget.yaml:9 ('guaranteed daily minimum'), defeating the floors' failover-headroom purpose; this is exactly the T8 contract-vs-implementation class. Latent: rebalance() has zero callers (cron.py:124 calls only allocate(); the unwired-caller issue is gap3-01, a distinct defect, not a duplicate). confirmed.title: publish_signal dedup falsely reports published=True for previously REJECTED signals after cache eviction
confirmed.claim: _remember() adds every terminal response -- including risk_rejected (:402), trade_rejected (:423,:440) and backend_unavailable (:357) -- to the unbounded _seen_signal_ids set, but the response cache is a 50-entry FIFO (:279-282). When a signal re-fires after its cached response was evicted, step 3 hits the 'seen but cache miss' branch and synthesizes published=True / reason 'deduped' -- telling the calling agent a trade was published when it was in fact rejected and never booked. Additionally, dedup-of-rejections makes a rejection permanent for the process lifetime: a BUY rejected for insufficient_cash can never be retried this session even after cash frees up, because the same signal_id short-circuits at step 3.
confirmed.evidence:
signals_server.py:343-348:
            # Cache miss but seen -- synthesize a minimal dedup response.
            resp = self._empty_response(signal_id=signal_id)
            resp["published"] = True
            resp["deduped"] = True
            resp["reason"] = "deduped"
            return resp
signals_server.py:400-403:

confirmed.basis: handoff/current/research_brief_75.0.md T6 rule 3 (tool responses must be truthful/high-signal; Anthropic writing-tools-for-agents source #7). False success on a trade-execution tool is the silent-corruption class the project's anti-leniency rules target.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Only _remember() successful publishes (published=True), or store the terminal outcome alongside the id (e.g. _seen_signal_ids -> dict id->reason) and synthesize the TRUE prior outcome with published=False for rejections. Never fabricate published=True on a cache miss.
confirmed.verify_reason: Evidence verified line-for-line: _remember() (:271-282) adds every terminal outcome -- backend_unavailable (:357), risk_rejected (:402), trade_rejected (:423/:440) -- to the unbounded _seen_signal_ids set (:79) while the response cache is a 50-entry FIFO (:81, :279-282). The cache-HIT dedup path (:335-342) truthfully replays the prior response (published=False for rejections), but after eviction the seen-but-cache-miss branch (:343-348) fabricates published=True / reason "deduped" regardless of the true prior outcome, inverting a recorded rejection. publish_signal is exposed as MCP tool mcp__pyfinagent-signals__publish_signal, so this false success reaches the agent layer in a long-running server. Not fixed, and not a duplicate: phase-69 register items on signals_server (avg_entry_price currency mix, held-ticker guard) are a different defect. Two corrections to the finding's framing: (a) the synthesized response carries trade_executed=False, so it claims publication, not a booked trade -- blast radius is a misleading status flag, not phantom position state; (b) the "rejection permanent for process lifetime" sub-claim is scoped -- _signal_id (:229-246) hashes ticker|date|signal|confidence-bucket, so only the identical same-day/same-bucket re-fire is blocked, and remembering backend_unavailable is explicitly intentional per the comment at :351-352. The fabricated published=True on cache miss, however, is clearly unintentional (contradicts the truthful cache-hit behavior and the anti-leniency return-shape rules cited in the file's own docstrings). P2 is honest: latent (requires 50+ unique signal ids between fire and re-fire in one process), no direct money movement, but a real truthfulness defect on a trade-adjacent tool. Suggested fix (remember outcome, never fabricate published=True) is correct.

confirmed.title: Risk Judge parse-failure fallback fails OPEN to APPROVE_REDUCED while the Moderator's equivalent fails safe to HOLD
confirmed.claim: When the Risk Judge's output cannot be parsed as JSON, run_risk_debate substitutes a synthetic verdict with decision='APPROVE_REDUCED', 3% position and risk_adjusted_confidence 0.5 -- i.e. the risk gate approves a trade whose risk verdict was never actually rendered. Mitigated by Gemini response_schema on the judge call (rare parse failures) but the Claude/LLMClient path passes the same Gemini-only config without schema enforcement.

confirmed.title: MetaCoordinator.gather_health never populates the interval gates and maps benchmark_beat_rate onto 'agent_accuracy'
